data.title: AstraZeneca to invest £11bn in China after snubbing Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca has unveiled plans to invest $15bn (£11bn) in China just months after dropping hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in Britain.

The Cambridge-based company announced the investment, which will be used to expand its medical manufacturing and research capabilities, during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing. The Prime Minister hailed the financing as a deal that would help to support British jobs back home.

But the announcement follows a period of frosty relations between the Government and AstraZeneca which has seen the drugmaker threaten to halt investment in Britain in a row over NHS pricing.

The pharmaceutical giant, which is Britain’s biggest listed company, has abandoned plans for a £200m expansion of its Cambridge research site and £450m overhaul of its Liverpool vaccine factory.

The deal may also raise eyebrows given that five of AstraZeneca’s staff were detained by authorities in China in 2024 amid claims of potential illegal activity. The president of its China division was subsequently placed under investigation by local police, with no further details provided.

Astra is the largest drugs company in China, which is the company’s second-biggest market after the US.

Separately, AstraZeneca also announced on Friday that it had struck a deal worth as much as $18.2bn with China’s CSPC Pharmaceuticals.

Under the tie-up, AstraZeneca will have the global rights to sell CSPC’s obesity drugs outside China.

CSPC is currently developing once-monthly weight-loss jabs. The biggest weight-loss treatments currently require people to inject themselves once a week.

In total, if all of CSPC’s eight obesity drugs make it to market, AstraZeneca said the deal could be worth as much as $18.2bn – including future sales of the weight-loss treatments.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the company’s chief executive, is one of a clutch of Western business leaders who are occasionally summoned for meetings with Xi Jinping, the Chinese premier.

Sir Pascal said on Thursday: “Today’s landmark investment of $15bn begins an exciting next chapter for AstraZeneca in China, which has become a critical contributor to scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.”

The “landmark” move was recognition of China’s growing scientific capabilities in areas that could help unlock new treatments for cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others. It will be focused on sites in Bejing, Shanghai, Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao.

Sir Keir said: “Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements.

“AstraZeneca’s expansion and leadership in China will help the British manufacturer continue to grow – supporting thousands of UK jobs.”

Astra has been at loggerheads with the Government over how much the NHS pays for medicines.

The company and its rivals have argued that the way the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) evaluates “value for money” on medicines is flawed and deprives patients of potential breakthrough treatments.

For instance, Nice previously rejected Enhertu, Astra’s breast cancer drug.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers last summer after failing to strike an agreement.

But in December, ministers announced a new deal with the US that saw Nice agree to pay up to 25pc more for treatments, a move aimed at protecting the UK life sciences industry.

data.title: Reform’s housing chief was right about Grenfell bureaucracy
data.description:
It was neither diplomatic, appropriate nor well-judged. Simon Dudley, Reform UK’s Housing spokesman, probably should not have casually added that “everyone dies in the end” after making the argument that the Grenfell fire has made building regulations far too cumbersome.

Yet, his essential point was surely right. In the aftermath of the tragedy, we have imposed so much red tape on every building project that the diggers and cement mixers can never start work.

In reality, if Reform wants to genuinely change the way Britain is governed, then it needs to embrace some harsh truths – and not fire people for simply stating the obvious.

Dudley is a rarity in British politics; someone who actually knows what he is talking about. A former executive at Homes England and the Ebbsfleet Development Corporation, he was appointed by Nigel Farage’s upstart party to lead an urgent review of the country’s “building crisis”.

Unfortunately, no one bothered telling him the one cardinal rule of the Westminster bubble is that everyone should stick to the polite fiction that higher taxes and more regulations are the answer to every problem.

In an interview with Inside Housing magazine, he argued that, in the wake of Grenfell, the pendulum on regulation had “swung too far the wrong way” and that “you can try to minimise excesses, but bad things do happen”.

He finished with the observation that while Grenfell was a “tragedy” but “sadly, you know, everyone dies in the end, adding “it’s just how you go, right?”

The predictable outrage arrived right on cue. Steve Reed, the Housing Secretary, called the remarks “shameful” and the Green Party’s Siân Berry said they were “truly abhorrent”.

Of course, Dudley’s words may well have been poorly chosen. We probably didn’t need the reminder that we are all mortal. And yet he was also completely right.

The building industry has collapsed and post-Grenfell over-regulation is largely to blame for that. Figures released in January showed that building in London, where new homes are most needed, has fallen by 84pc over the last decade.

Only this week, Rob Perrins, the chief executive of property developer Berkeley, suggested London had become uninvestable because of the taxes and regulations piled onto developers, singling out the building safety levy for particular criticism. For England as a whole, new building starts are now 57pc below the 2023 level, while starts on “affordable homes” are down by 12pc year-on-year.

Why? It is not that the country has suddenly decided we didn’t want homes any more. It is the regulations that have made it impossible. These include requirements for all buildings more than 18 metres to have a second staircase to mitigate the fire risk.

It might sound desirable, but it hugely increases the cost as well as reducing the amount of floorspace that can be sold or rented out.

The Building Safety Regulator, according to a 2024 report, is only approving 14pc of “remediation” plans, meaning hugely costly documents have to be submitted with only a one in six chance of success. It is hardly surprising that developers don’t bother with those odds; they would have a better chance of making money at the race track. The list goes on and on.

The blunt truth is this. Until Britain can escape the suffocating “safety-ism” that stops anything from being built, there is no hope of the construction crisis ever being fixed.

More seriously, until we are willing to bring plain-speaking experts, with real-world experience, such as Dudley, into government and give them the freedom and power to start making things happen again, there is little hope of the economy ever reviving.

Reform could have ticked him off for the words, while backing the message. By firing Dudley, the party has surely raised doubts about whether it is serious about changing anything.

data.title: OpenAI blames Britain’s high energy prices as it halts flagship project
data.description:
The maker of ChatGPT has said Britain’s energy costs are too high as it suspended plans for data centre investments in the UK.

OpenAI said it was putting plans for a UK arm of its multibillion-dollar “Stargate” project on hold until power prices drop and conditions improve.

It marks a major setback to Sir Keir Starmer’s hopes of boosting growth through a data centre boom. The Government had previously trumpeted OpenAI’s plans for significant investment in Britain during Donald Trump’s state visit.

The $850bn (£634bn) American giant last year claimed that the Stargate UK project would strengthen the country’s “sovereign compute capabilities”. It said this would mean its AI models could “run on local computing power in the UK, for the UK”.

However, The Telegraph reported last week that the project had been delayed after it failed to hit a March deadline. OpenAI has cut spending plans globally.

The company said it continued to see “huge potential for the UK’s AI future”, adding: “London is home to our largest international research hub, and we support the Government’s ambition to be an AI leader.”

A spokesman said: “AI compute is foundational to that goal – we continue to explore Stargate UK and will move forward when the right conditions, such as regulation and the cost of energy, enable long-term infrastructure investment.

“In the meantime, we are investing in talent and expanding our local presence, while also delivering on the commitments under our [memorandum of understanding] with the Government to adopt frontier AI in UK public services.”

Britain suffers from the highest industrial electricity costs in the developed world, paying 63pc more than France and 27pc more than Germany, according to the International Energy Agency.

The average day-ahead price of electricity in Britain was about $104 per megawatt hour (MWh) over the past year, according to the agency. That compared with $74 per MWh in Japan, $64 in France and $43 in the United States.

Data centres, and particularly AI systems, are energy-intensive, making them vulnerable to soaring electricity costs.

Microsoft said last year that grid connection delays were putting investments at risk.

Stargate UK was announced last September as a project with British data centre company Nscale and chip giant Nvidia. OpenAI said it would involve a number of sites, starting with Cobalt Park, an existing data centre site in the North East.

The announcement led the Government to declare the North East one of its “AI growth zones”, which benefit from planning and power advantages.

George Osborne, the former chancellor, joined OpenAI last December to pursue data centre investments outside the US.

However, the company recently reduced spending commitments, saying it expected to spend around $600bn by 2030, compared to previous estimates of $1.4tn.

It is understood that discussions are ongoing about an investment.

A government spokesman said: “Our AI sector has attracted more than £100bn in private investment since the Government took office, with the sector growing 23 times faster than the wider economy last year. That is delivering the jobs and opportunities hard-working people deserve.

“Our focus is on continuing to create the right conditions for investment in the UK’s AI and data centre infrastructure. We are continuing to work with OpenAI and other leading AI companies to strengthen UK compute capacity.”

data.title: A ‘1960s hellhole’ bungalow transformed into a dream home
data.description:
When Elizabeth Taylor stumbled across an outdated 1960s bungalow hidden away in the West Sussex village where she grew up, she saw beyond its shabby façade. In its place came a vivid vision shaped by her travels along America’s East Coast, from New England to the Florida Keys, where she had fallen for the understated charm of clapboard houses. Painted white with dormer windows and wide porches, often with a swing, these rather romantic homes were typically set close to water. The only thing linking this property to those in America was its coastal setting, a small river running through its grounds and Elizabeth’s ability to imagine a complete transformation.

At the time, Elizabeth, a wedding photographer, was living nearby in a 1930s semi – her third renovation – and had no plans to move. But when she spotted the bungalow in an estate agent’s window, her curiosity was piqued. A viewing revealed a house hidden at the end of a private road, screened by towering leylandii and, inside, a range of Sixties features such as York-stone fireplaces, vinyl finishes, Artex ceilings and veneered wardrobes. It wasn’t the prettiest of houses, but it was detached, with a big garden. “It certainly wasn’t my dream home, but it had scope to go every which way, including up,” says Elizabeth. “I could picture it surrounded by azaleas, magnolias and camellias.” Six weeks later, in 2012, she had sold her home and moved in.

Decorating in stages

As Elizabeth had a young child and a limited budget, progress was gradual. “I started with the basics, just to make it liveable,” she says. A new bathroom was top of the list – particularly after her son discovered the walk-in bath would flood the room if it was opened mid-soak. Other rooms were stripped back and refreshed. “It smelt of the Sixties for about four years, until we finally got all the wallpaper and carpet out,” Elizabeth recalls.

While these early years didn’t bring about much change, they gave her time to think. “I’d previously renovated older homes with strong architectural features to steer design choices, but it was clear this project was going to be different,” she says. “I ended up concluding that I would just have to trust my instinct.”

In 2014, her now-husband Jamie Taylor, a builder and carpenter, entered the picture, and plans began to take shape. “Without him we’d probably still be living in a 1960s hellhole,” she laughs.

Reimagining the layout

The original layout needed rethinking. “It was completely disjointed,” says Elizabeth. She and Jamie worked with an architect to develop plans that balanced ambition with budget: “I thought having lots of options was a good thing, but it just sent me down endless Pinterest rabbit holes – unable to make decisions.” Rather than extending dramatically outwards, they eventually chose to build upwards to echo the American aesthetic that she so admired. The plans included loft and garage conversions, a modest extension and the addition of a veranda. Extending the front by just 1.5 metres to accommodate a hallway and porch proved transformative. “It was a small change, but it made a huge difference,” says Elizabeth.

Inside, the footprint remained similar, but awkward spaces were opened up. The cramped kitchen-diner, garage access and an odd outhouse were combined into one generous room, now the family’s day-to-day living space. “All those little nooks disappeared and suddenly the house made sense.”

Creating a budget kitchen with character

With the layout established, creating a kitchen with personality was central to Elizabeth’s vision, but staying within budget was a challenge. “Everything I loved was far too expensive,” she says. The solution came via eBay, where she found some old-school science cabinets. The cabinet doors had flat fronts but shaker-style backs, so they were simply reversed and rehung. Elizabeth then repainted each piece by hand, preserving the original lettering and brass fittings. “At one point I was using a tiny make-up brush to get into all the details,” she recalls. Glazed cabinets from the same find were fitted above. “Everyone thought I was mad putting in so many hours, but it turned out well and was a real bargain.”

Professionally fitted new quartz worktops added a touch of luxury, and they kept costs down elsewhere by reusing a sink from the old utility room. The space was designed to be sociable as well as practical, with a sofa, a salvaged island to gather around and a large dining table. Scaffold boards were lightly hand-sanded and varnished for flooring, and much of the furniture was sourced second-hand. “By using reclaimed materials and pre-loved furniture, we’ve created real character here,” says Elizabeth, who credits this approach with helping to shape and guide her overall design direction.

A resourceful approach

The former garage became a bedroom for Elizabeth’s son, Jack (now 21), with a vaulted ceiling and a mezzanine. The space between the kitchen and garage was enclosed to form a hallway and bathroom, creating a self-contained suite. Reclaimed scaffold boards were again used for flooring and stairs, while iron spindles from a car boot sale form the balustrade. “Scaffold boards have been a real hero material,” says Elizabeth. “They’re incredibly versatile and bring a warmth and texture that new materials just don’t have.”

The loft was also converted into a spacious bedroom with a dormer bathroom, and retained its dramatic vaulted ceiling.

Throughout, resourcefulness was key: “I wanted a high-end finish that reflected my taste, but we didn’t always have the budget,” says Elizabeth. Reclaimed materials, second-hand finds and Jamie’s craftsmanship kept costs manageable: only around 5 per cent of the materials and furnishings used in the project are new. The work was often done in stages, and sometimes they had to pause and wait for the time, money or motivation to continue.

Over the years, the total cost has reached around £250,000. “We never set a strict budget,” says Elizabeth. “We just had a vision and worked towards it whenever we could.” Part of the vision was to create a seamless connection between inside and out, achieved through the use of two complementary shades of cream across both the clapboard exterior and the interior.

Transforming the garden

Their attention then turned to the overgrown garden, beginning with the removal of the front hedging. “While it kept us completely private, it wasn’t the view we wanted,” says Elizabeth. “Taking it down was the best thing we could have done.” The change revealed the riverbank and mature trees, including elegant weeping willows, creating a stronger connection to the landscape.

This new outlook helped to define the vision for the rest of the garden. An old summer house was replaced with a purpose-built photography studio made from larch wood and reclaimed Crittall windows, while a greenhouse was created from salvaged materials. Landscaping introduced structure, with new paths, defined areas and a central patio. The result is a garden that feels open yet secluded.

“It finally feels like the garden belongs to the house,” says Elizabeth; and she feels she belongs there too. The project has become a deeply personal expression of everything she loves, and it seems a home made with love is a recipe for success.

See more on Instagram at @thetaylormadehouse

data.title: Six reasons why lists have ruined my life
data.description:
The life of a writer in 2026 is plagued by all sorts of woes. AI is the spectre haunting would-be novel manuscripts; the short-form video slop of TikTok and Instagram is rendering the written word as antique as a landline telephone. Yet there’s one monster which is even more insidious: the listicle, that part-list part-article hybrid which has colonised magazine inches and online news articles.

  1. This Frankenstein’s monster of a literary form surely poses an existential threat to our already under-taxed attention spans – but I can’t help but wonder if it might have more apocalyptic ramifications. Could it be spreading beyond the containment zone of (so-called) journalism and into the world at large?
  2. I’ve found I now can’t go to the cinema without wondering where the film I am about to see will fit within my own personal pantheon of “Top Five Best Films of All Time” – or subcategories including “Top Three Best Films in Which an Australian Tries to Do a Northern Accent” and “The Most Gratuitously Sexual Adaptations of a Victorian Novel”. (Emerald Fennell, call me – I have a Peacock listicle award for your Wuthering Heights).
  3. In a similar vein, I now can’t read a book, at all. It’s simply impossible to parse the long paragraphs of black-and-white hierographic squiggles without the reassuring appearance of prose broken up into numbered segments. I’m having to plough all my profits from writing into my new venture: a publishing company that re-writes classics into easy-to-read list form. Our first title, Thirteen Reasons Why War Sucks and Peace Is Good will be published this summer. (Other titles in production are Three Reasons Men Love Boats, formerly by Jerome K. Jerome and Five Slaughterhouses to Visit, previously the work of Kurt Vonnegut).
  4. The Great God Listicle has even got his claws into my home life. Conversations with my husband now have to follow the law of the list. If I make an opening sally with “Six Reasons It Would Be Really Great If You Could Put a Wash On”, he replies with “Eight Suspicious Mugs You Have Left in the Sink”. And it’s not just us: I recently had a catch-up with a friend who regaled the group with the listicle “Top 100 Most Annoying Housemate Habits”.
  5. Even my internal monologue has begun to present itself in listicle form. Struggling to get to sleep last night, my brain presented me with the charming news-flash, “Four Reasons You’re an Idiot for Scrolling on Your Phone Before Bed”, quickly followed up with “Five Reasons You’re Going to Be Tired Tomorrow”.
  6. Where does this listicle’s domination end? As the format erodes our literacy and brain power, will there soon be a mandate that requires us all to start speaking in short, easy-to-read sentences with numbered bullet points? Will we have to shout a clickbait title first, and then demand our listeners pay us a fiver to hear the rest? I for one can’t wait for the Government to start communicating with us in catchy pieces like “Five Ways the UK Is Heading for a Recession” and “Three Nifty Tricks to Survive World War Three”. The paragraph is dead, all hail the mighty listicle.

data.title: City raises the alarm over damaging new Brussels rules
data.description:
The City has raised the alarm about damaging new rules from Brussels that threaten to raise costs for British banks.

New regulations will restrict the ability of banks outside the EU to offer banking services to companies based in the bloc.

Banks looking to provide those services, such as lending and cash management, will have to set up subsidiaries within the bloc.

Industry groups warned that the new rules, which are scheduled to take effect by the end of this year, will mean higher costs for banks in the UK and elsewhere.

There are fears that the changes could push up the cost of rearmament in both the UK and the EU, given that many defence companies’ operations stretch across both the UK and the EU.

It is understood that lobby groups have been in discussions with defence companies that might be affected and are raising concerns with other businesses in the EU.

Scott Devine, the associate director of TheCityUK, the lobby group for the Square Mile, said that the regulations would be a drag on the UK’s financial sector and contrasted with Rachel Reeves’s ambition for closer ties between the UK and the EU.

“In terms of new cross-border bank lending, there’ll be a limit there, and that will almost certainly have an impact on a number of UK-based financial services firms,” Mr Devine said.

“This is one of the more egregious examples of that kind of policymaking, which we had hoped we’d see less of given that there has been more of a rapprochement at the senior political level.”

The new rules are widely believed to have been introduced at the behest of France. A French member of the European Parliament added the measure at the last minute to a broader set of EU banking rules.

Mr Devine said the new policy was the latest example of the EU attempting to use its influence to take parts of the UK’s financial sector following a trade deal that was agreed in 2020.

“This is another measure which is part of the ratcheting effects which we’ve seen since the EU-UK Trade and Cooperation Agreement was agreed upon, in which the EU is focusing financial services policy based on forced relocation, rather than on market efficiency,” Mr Devine added.

A recent paper by UK Finance and a number of other trade groups warned that the planned changes could mean higher banking costs for European companies and the possibility of increased risks to financial stability.

Nikhil Rathi, the head of the Financial Conduct Authority, warned in a speech last October that the measures threaten to limit the scope for investment into European defence firms.

“Just as we deepen security cooperation across Europe, proposed EU cross-border branching restrictions and patchwork rules raise the cost of precisely the investment our EU colleagues say they want,” Mr Rathi said.

The Treasury said it would “continue to monitor” the EU’s position to “understand potential impacts” of the new rules.

“We regularly engage with EU counterparts and financial services stakeholders and continue to monitor the EU’s position to understand potential impacts,” a spokesman said.

A spokesman for the European Commission said: “Banks wishing to offer banking services in the EU from a third country must set up a branch or a subsidiary in the EU and apply for authorisation.

“This clarification is particularly relevant given the evolving landscape of cross-border financial services, including cases where jurisdictions previously operating within the EU framework are now treated as third countries. This makes a clear and harmonised EU regime essential.”

data.title: If Labour is serious about growth, it must open up the North Sea
data.description:
One consequence of the current surge in global energy prices is that it has turned the spotlight onto Britain’s policy with regard to its unexploited supplies of fossil fuels. Sixty-five leading climate scientists recently wrote an open letter to the Financial Times arguing against new drilling in the North Sea. They said that this would imperil the fight against climate change while bringing no economic benefit. Do they have a point?

The argument primarily concerns the Jackdaw and Rosebank fields. These two fields are not covered by the ban on new licenses since their applications had already been submitted when Labour took office.

The issue here is not climate science which, despite the claim that everything in this subject is settled, remains controversial. But we don’t need to get into that. Rather, the issue here is about economics.

The climate authors advance several arguments. One is that 90pc of the energy reserves in the North Sea have already been exploited. This is true but so what? Why does this mean that we should leave the remaining 10pc unexploited?

Another is that it is not worth opening up new supplies from the North Sea because this would have little or no effect on the prices of gas and oil here because these are set globally. This is true but allowing extra domestic production would create more jobs in the parts of the country that have suffered from the rundown of the North Sea industry and produce increased tax revenues.

The UK’s trade deficit would be correspondingly reduced. So long as this activity is profitable, it will bring benefit to the UK economy. And the people to judge whether or not it is profitable are the energy companies, which want to have the chance to exploit these two North Sea fields.

Indeed, it would be wrong to use the availability of domestic supplies to keep prices down artificially. Domestic consumers of energy should face world prices for energy. In that way they will be incentivised to minimise their consumption of it. But profitable domestic energy supply can improve UK economic performance such that the Government could give relief to consumers in other ways, rather than artificially keeping the price down.

The great irony about our restrictions on UK fossil fuel production is that it is not unambiguously reducing emissions. We import 35pc of our gas from Norway. The fact that this gas is produced there rather than in the UK does nothing to reduce carbon emissions. Indeed, this imported gas may generate higher emissions than our own domestically produced gas. This is the height of absurdity.

Some defenders of the current policy claim that renewable energy is cheaper. If that were true, than we surely ought to rush headlong towards replacing all fossil fuel energy with renewables. But it isn’t. Admittedly, it is true that the marginal cost of renewable energy is often lower than for fossil fuel produced energy. But this ignores the fact that because of the intermittency of renewables – sometimes the wind does not blow and/or the sun does not shine – there has to be huge and expensive duplicate capacity.

An argument that we hear often is that the UK’s energy policy is setting an example to the world. This betrays an exaggerated view of the UK’s importance in both global energy markets and in the world more generally. Indeed, it harks back to the days of empire. We are now only a medium-sized economy.

Admittedly, there is scheduled to be a major international conference on climate change later this month in Columbia. The UK will be one of about 50 countries represented. Allowing new drilling in the North Sea could potentially cause the UK government considerable embarrassment at this forum.

But so what? What is the market price of embarrassment? The big boys in global energy are the US and China. Needless to say, they are continuing to consume and produce large amounts of energy from fossil fuels. For all the effect our policy has on them, we might as well wave a union jack over the North Sea. (Come to think of it, though, this might cause distress and therefore
requires a trigger warning.)

Allowing drilling in the Jackdaw and Rosebank fields would not in itself transform UK energy supply or the economy more generally. But it would be a start. The Government repeatedly tells us that its overriding ambition is to deliver decent economic growth for the UK. And within this, it sees manufacturing as key. Yet the UK has some of the highest industrial energy prices in the developed world. They have recently been three times America’s. Scarce wonder, then, that UK manufacturing has been in the doldrums.

But the damage does not stop there. The Government is relying on the UK’s prominent position in AI to deliver decent economic growth. Yet AI is a huge consumer of energy. Worryingly, OpenAI has recently announced that it is halting its UK data centre project, citing high energy cost and concerns about regulation.

By wanting to secure cheap energy for the UK, this does not mean that we necessarily reject the whole policy agenda against global warming. The issue is about the pace of change, the cost, and how a medium-sized economy should behave when the big boys are setting the pace. Even climate change sceptics would surely acknowledge that it would be good if we can eventually wean ourselves off reliance on fossil fuels, because so much of the world’s supplies of gas and oil are located in places with hostile governments (Russia) or in areas of instability (the Middle East).

Yet this does not mean that we should impoverish ourselves in the process.

Roger Bootle is senior independent adviser to Capital Economics and a senior fellow at Policy Exchange. roger.bootle@capitaleconomics.com

data.title: West Midlands Police makes £1m by ‘harassing’ lorry drivers
data.description:
West Midlands Police has come under fire for making more than £1m by “harassing” lorry drivers and forcing them to pay for escorts.

The force has increased its income from road haulage companies 70-fold over the past five years from £15,000 to more than £1m, according to data obtained by the Construction Plant-hire Association (CPA).

Police have powers to require an escort for unusually large or heavy lorries which pass through their area while making deliveries, including those made to HS2 sites.

Lord Attlee, who went undercover to investigate the force, compared the tactics used by West Midlands Police to “ones that you would expect to see used by a corrupt police force in a slowly developing country”.

A qualified lorry driver himself from his days in the Royal Electrical and Mechanical Engineers, the Tory peer travelled in the cab of a lorry on a route known to be targeted regularly by West Midlands Police while posing as a driving assessor from the haulage firm.

He told Parliament that he witnessed officers “harassing” drivers by “deliberately misquoting or misapplying regulations, and prohibiting vehicle movements when there is no power to do so”.

“Such excessive and overzealous checks are interfering with the smooth running of the heavy haulage industry, which is racking up significant extra costs in all the wasted time spent responding to these demands,” he added.

If officers decide the load needs to be escorted by them, there is nothing to prevent them refusing permission until the lorry company agrees to pay the force sums measured in thousands of pounds.

Lord Attlee also told The Telegraph that he believed West Midlands Police had been charging haulage companies its standard six-hour overtime rate several times in one day.

This means the same group of police officers may have billed those companies several times over for the same amount of work, he said.

‘Disruption and stress across the industry’

The boss of one CPA member company, who spoke on condition of anonymity for fear of police retaliation, said: “Operators are also being pushed to request police escorts when they are not needed. If an escort is requested, the cost is around £2,500 just to travel through the West Midlands area.

“This is creating a huge administrative burden and will cause serious disruption and stress across the industry,” added the executive, who explained that some deliveries were only worth £2,000 – meaning police demands for money more than doubled the price paid by the customer.

Steven Mulholland, chief executive of the CPA, said: “This isn’t about safety – it’s about misinterpretation and incentives that are putting HS2 and major infrastructure projects at risk.

“You simply cannot deliver HS2 or major rail and construction projects if the machinery needed to build them is held up by regional policy drift.

“We need an urgent return to a single, nationally consistent interpretation of the rules, so compliant movements are treated the same way in every part of the country.”

A West Midlands Police spokesman said that the force tries to “foster positive relationships with haulage operators”, doing so “without unnecessary penalties”.

“Demand for abnormal loads across the region is increasing, fuelled by a thriving industry and major infrastructure projects such as HS2 and developments around M42 Junction 5A,” the spokesman said.

“Last year, we assessed 171,565 notifications. Of these, 13,354 notifications were reviewed as they potentially required police support due to the route, size or nature of the load.

“A total of 706 were escorted by our officers, which equates to 0.41 per cent of movement notifications.

“We only ever seek details that are relevant and outlined in legislation, and vehicle movements are only ever prohibited when they are assessed as unlawful.”

data.title: World not prepared for interest rate shock, warns JP Morgan boss
data.description:
The global economy will be rocked by an even bigger interest rate shock than expected from war in Iran, JP Morgan’s boss has warned.

Jamie Dimon, head of the world’s biggest bank, said on Monday that borrowing costs could be forced higher as the conflict in the Middle East pushes up oil prices.

“Because of the war in Iran, we face the potential for significant ongoing oil and commodity price shocks, along with the reshaping of global supply chains,” Mr Dimon said in his annual letter to shareholders.

This “may lead to stickier inflation and ultimately higher interest rates than markets currently expect”, he added.

The comments will surprise US traders, who have not priced in any interest rate increase from the Federal Reserve this year or next.

The US benchmark rate is currently at 3.5pc to 3.75pc. In the UK, traders are betting that the Bank of England will lift rates twice from 3.75pc to 4.25pc this year.

However, Mr Dimon, one of the most influential voices on Wall Street, suggests that even higher rate increases could be on the cards.

Mr Trump has repeatedly called on the Federal Reserve to cut borrowing costs faster, taking aim at Jerome Powell, the outgoing Fed chairman, for not cutting rates.

Mr Dimon’s warning underlines the risks to the global economy from the war and fears the conflict will sap the economy for months to come, even if a ceasefire is agreed.

In his 48-page shareholder letter, Mr Dimon also suggested that Mr Trump may struggle to end the war he started and warned about the growing cost of energy.

“War is the realm of uncertainty, as each side in a war determines what it wants to do (as is often said, ‘the enemy gets a vote’), and these conflicts involve many countries.

“Nations that are heavily dependent upon imported energy are already seeing the effects. And it’s not just energy, it’s commodity products that are byproducts of oil and gas, like fertiliser and helium.”

He added: “Given our complex global supply chains, countries are experiencing disruptions in shipbuilding, food and farming, among others.

“The outcome of current geopolitical events may very well be the defining factor in how the future global economic order unfolds – then again, it may not.”

Mr Trump is currently embroiled in a legal tussle with Mr Dimon and JP Morgan, having sued the bank earlier this year for $5bn (£3.7bn), claiming his account was shut down for political reasons. JP Morgan has said the lawsuit has “no merit”.

The banking chief also took a swipe at Mark Carney, the Canadian prime minister, who has urged so-called middle-power countries to come together.

“Middle powers must act together because if we’re not at the table, we’re on the menu,” Mr Carney told Davos in January, as Mr Trump threatened to seize Greenland.

However, Mr Dimon warned that both the West and America would be worse off from such an approach.

“Some political leaders have said that there is a ‘rupture’ between America and the Western world – that the red lines have been crossed and there is no return to the prior system. I completely disagree,” he said.

Mr Dimon instead called on US leaders to “fix the current situation” and work together more closely.

The JP Morgan chief hit the headlines earlier this year when he warned about private credit, saying more “cockroaches” would emerge from the system.

In his letter, he said that losses from the sector would be “higher than expected” when the market turns negative.

data.title: Hope for run-down Boots as billionaire owner’s wife exits
data.description:
The British high street is experiencing its biggest cull in years.

Already reeling from a series of costly tax raids at the hands of Rachel Reeves, tough Christmas trading has proven to be the nail in the coffin for a string of struggling household names, including Claire’s, Russell & Bromley and LK Bennett. More high-profile casualties are expected in the coming weeks.

Amid the continued hollowing out of town centres, one high street stalwart remains: Boots.

Outposts of the lipstick-to-prescriptions business can still be found across the country, but many are in a sad state. After its exit from the stock market in 2007, Boots has spent the last two decades being starved of investment.

With serial dealmaker Stefano Pessina leading the charge, Boots has been allowed to dwindle – first under the ownership of private equity’s original Barbarians at the Gate, KKR, then as a forgotten overseas outpost of American pharmacy giant Walgreens.

Now Boots is in the hands of another private equity house, Sycamore Partners, after the New York-based outfit took Walgreens off the public markets last summer. Employees must fear yet more decline.

However, a shake-up at the very top of the company may signal a revival for one of Britain’s best-loved and most important retailers.

After leading the pharmacy giant for almost a decade, Boots chief executive Ornella Barra is stepping down to become chair. She replaces Pessina, who is also her business partner and husband, and is believed to still own half of the business at the ripe age of 84.

At the same time, managing director Anthony Hemmerdinger is being handed a much bigger role running Boots, including oversight of the No7 beauty brand – long regarded as one of several jewels in the stable.

The management reshuffle is viewed internally, and in the City, as another major step towards setting Boots free from its ownership purgatory and returning it to the UK stock market.

City sources said a float is still the main long-term ambition of the owners, and more likely to occur next year than in 2026.

A former executive said a spin-off of Boots “was always the plan” and the boardroom changes were “a step towards that”. The break-up of Walgreens into five standalone entities – Boots among them – shortly after its £8bn takeover last year was seen as the first real step.

The prospect of Boots being groomed for a spectacular public markets comeback under new leadership will raise hopes of a long overdue makeover of its unloved shops. Shoppers will be hoping that it also means a halt to any further closures.

The sale to Sycamore came after Boots axed 300 stores across the UK, drastically reducing its footprint from 2,200 to around 1,900 branches. Boots also shut 48 opticians in 2020, and had kickstarted a store closure plan the previous year that ended with more than 200 outlets being given the chop.

The status of Boots as more than just your typical bog-standard retailer means its future garners more attention than others.

Armed with Britain’s best-selling skincare brand No 7, the chain believes it can continue to compete in the booming beauty market despite the excitement surrounding the arrival of LVMH-backed French phenomenon Sephora in Britain, as well as other upstarts.

Amid the glamour of expensive lipstick and eyelash-thickening mascara, it is easy to forget that Boots is an integral part of the country’s healthcare system. Without its sprawling network of pharmacists, thousands of people would struggle to get their vital medicines and daily pills.

Its last round of store closures was accompanied by an apologetic statement explaining that the decision was “not taken lightly” and that in all cases there was an alternative store less than three miles away.

Yet that didn’t prevent an outcry in some affected towns, with locals complaining about being forced to endure long queues at other pharmacies, which in some cases had led to delays in getting their prescriptions.

Boots bosses have long rejected the portrayal of a business stuck in retreat as lazy and outdated.

“The Boots Group is in great shape, both financially and operationally, with Boots UK having grown market share for 19 consecutive quarters,” Barra said as she stepped aside.

But that fact masks the two-speed nature of the business. A former executive said there are around 500 Boots shops that are “the powerhouse of the business” – the vast majority of which have already been revamped.

However, the rest are much smaller shops, which are vital to the surrounding communities but cost “between £50,000 and £100,000” to refurbish with no resulting “uplift” in turnover, making it hard to justify the outlay.

“Pharmacies are really difficult to make money in now, because of what the Government pays per prescription,” another ex-director said. In November, the Department of Health and Social Care announced that NHS prescription charges would be frozen for the second year running.

 Clive Black, analyst at Shore Capital, adds: “There has been immense profiling of a small number of new and modernised stores, gaining lots of capital expenditure and senior management attention, to show the newfound vitality of Boots.

“But the reality is that many Boots are drab, under-invested, and needing of an injection of elevated execution that British shoppers will notice across the board.”

Those who know the business well say Boots branches need to become even bigger healthcare hubs, so that they can alleviate pressure on local GPs by providing more of the services that you would usually go to your doctor for.

The chain already does private online doctor consultations, vaccinations and blood pressure checks, as well as specialised treatments for skin and hair loss. However, it is eyeing a larger role in diagnostics with “at-home” testing kits for conditions such as high cholesterol, vitamin deficiencies and food intolerances. These will be offered under the mantra of standing “shoulder to shoulder with the NHS”.

If the move is successful, it would justify spending more capital on its smaller stores, many of which struggle to turn a profit.

The former executive says: “The mantra…was always ‘We’re going to have a great Boots in every major town, and none that we’re ashamed of.’ I think that will continue.”

data.title: Ed Miliband overrules locals to approve Britain’s biggest solar farm
data.description:
Ed Miliband has approved Britain’s biggest solar farm despite objections from nearby residents who have likened it to Chernobyl.

The Springwell Solar Farm in Lincolnshire will have a capacity of 800 megawatts, enough to power some 180,000 homes a year, the Government said on Wednesday.

The development, proposed by EDF and Luminous Energy, is the 25th large solar farm approved by Labour since 2024 using planning powers that allow ministers to overrule communities when a project is deemed “nationally significant”.

It has proved divisive in Lincolnshire, where residents turned out in large numbers to object to the plans at various public hearings.

It will cover seven square miles of farmland in solar panels – an area 10 times greater than London’s Hyde Park – and affect 10 local villages and thousands of residents.

Residents claimed the loss of prime farmland and the effect on communities was too high a price to pay for the green energy the project would provide.

Local politicians had also accused Mr Miliband, the Energy Secretary, of having “made up his mind already” despite fears the scheme “would change the very nature of Lincolnshire”.

In 2024, Labour scrapped planning rules that blocked the construction of new solar farms on food-producing land.

Mr Miliband also designated large solar and wind farms as “nationally significant” schemes that planners should approve by default.

Marc Williams, of the Springwell Solar Farm Action Group, told Lincolnshire Live last May: “The community are so concerned about this ... Everyone’s against it, apart from those who will profit.

“We wouldn’t object to plans for a couple of hundred acres but this is vast. It will be an industrialised complex like Chernobyl.

“People will go for a drive and see nothing but panels.”

‘Bulldoze the blockers’

Ministers said the scheme would boost supplies of “home-grown” electricity at a time when the Middle East crisis had “exposed the cost of relying on fossil fuel markets Britain does not control”.

Michael Shanks, the energy minister, said: “We are driving further and faster for clean home-grown power that we control to protect the British people and bring down bills for good.

“It is crucial we learn the lessons of the conflict in the Middle East – solar is one of the cheapest forms of power available and is how we get off the roller-coaster of international fossil fuel markets and secure our own energy independence.”

Mr Miliband has been leading government efforts to make Britain’s electricity system run on 95pc clean power by 2030, vowing to “double down” on the Government’s net zero policies since the outbreak of the Iran war.

However, many of the solar and onshore wind farms proposed in rural areas of England are facing stiff resistance from local residents. Labour has repeatedly insisted it will push through green energy infrastructure despite this opposition.

data.title: How Sadiq Khan made London into a taxi driver’s worst nightmare
data.description:
It should be a straightforward journey that needn’t take too long – Liverpool Street to London Bridge station, two areas heavily populated with white-collar workers heading from the commuter belts into the capital for their careers.

However, those opting to drive the short one-and-a-half miles find themselves forced to go a more roundabout way – through Whitechapel, in order to avoid the bus gate at Bishopsgate, which prevents motorists (yet allows buses and bikes) from accessing the road.

So, instead of taking around five minutes in the car, it takes between 20 and 30 minutes. Realistically, it would be quicker for drivers to swap four wheels for two legs.

Bishopsgate is just one of the many roads in the capital that have become increasingly unwelcoming to motorists, with a recent survey conducted by TomTom, the satnav company, finding that London is the slowest-driving capital city in the world.

It takes an average of three minutes and 38 seconds to travel one measly kilometre (0.6 miles) in the centre of the city. The analysis also found drivers lost an average of 141 hours stuck in rush hour traffic in 2025.

This glacial pace is annoying when you are trying to get somewhere in a hurry, to attend an important meeting, perhaps, or to make it to the theatre before curtain call. However, for the black cab drivers of London, increasingly congested, slow and difficult-to-navigate roads are leading to a significant loss of earnings, and even putting their livelihoods – and the sight of the London icon – at risk of extinction.

As of late 2025, the number of licensed drivers had fallen to around 14,800, down from more than 22,500 a decade earlier.

For Steve Cox, who became a black cab driver in 2022, navigating the streets of London has become increasingly difficult. He cites low-traffic neighbourhoods (LTNs) – areas where roads are closed to traffic to allow cyclists and pedestrians to take priority – as one of the main contributors to the capital’s slow roads.

“What used to be a 15-minute journey can now take you up to an hour,” the 54-year-old explains. “Tottenham Court Road is severely restricted for motorists between 8am and 7pm, which are some of your peak hours as a black cab.

“If you want to go from one side of Tottenham Court Road to the other to drop a passenger, we have to do two different squares and go round several back roads. It’s just ridiculous.

“It’s another 15 to 20 minutes on the journey time, and probably another £15-£20 for the customer. I’m embarrassed to charge them. It’s unfair and leads to complaints that aren’t down to me – and results in fewer people trying to flag us down.”

While LTNs were initially implemented in London in the 1970s (although under a different name) to filter traffic in the Hackney area, they were implemented en masse in March 2020 under Mayor of London Sir Sadiq Khan’s plans to improve road safety, decrease air and noise pollution, and encourage “active travel” such as walking and cycling.

Now, it is thought four per cent of London’s nine-million-strong population lives in an LTN.

These have made navigating around the capital increasingly problematic; approximately 95 per cent of black cab drivers report that LTNs and poor road conditions make picking up and dropping off passengers difficult.

Cabbies also cite London’s new speed restrictions as a reason why the capital often remains at a standstill. According to Transport for London (TfL), more than half of London’s roads now have a 20mph limit. This includes all roads within London’s Congestion Charge zone, as well as major roads, such as the A107 corridor and the White Hart Roundabout.

While this was implemented to try and cut deaths and serious injuries, City Hall was previously told this change was “devastating to the taxi industry” by Steve McNamara, general secretary of the Licensed Taxi Drivers Association in 2024.

“I understand the need for 20mph zones around schools,” says Frank O’Beirne, who has been a black cab driver for 14 years. “However, places like Park Lane, which have been reduced from three lanes of traffic to just one in order for there to be a bus and cycling lane, is just madness – especially now we’re stuck only being able to go 20mph there.”

The 50-year-old adds: “While our meters do go up in traffic, it’s only incremental – we need to be moving to actually have decent fares. Customers get so fed up in traffic they just jump out.”

Sadiq Khan’s green crusade for London has reached a nadir with the highly criticised and controversial ultra-low emission zone (Ulez). Since 2018, all newly-licenced black cabs need to be “zero-emission capable”, while the maximum operating age for older, more polluting diesel taxis has been reduced from 15 years to 12.

Cox says some older cabbies have been forced into an early retirement. “I know men in their 60s who have had their cab for 10 years and just didn’t see the point of carrying on investing so much money in a new car, and have gone and got jobs in Tesco instead.

“That and the stress of trying to earn enough money to keep you afloat is enough to push someone into another job. I don’t blame people for saying: ‘I don’t need this anymore.’”

It’s of little surprise, then, that a quarter of all black cabs on the roads before the pandemic are no longer in operation. Renting a traditional black cab, or Hackney carriage, is not cheap. Cox pays £360 a week to rent his vehicle, while others pay £400 and beyond. That doesn’t include the additional outgoings for cabbies, such as the mandatory two MOTs a year; while cab drivers don’t pay this, it does result in a loss of earnings as you can’t work while your vehicle is being serviced.

Cox says he needs to earn more than £3,000 a month just to break even on the investments he’s made to become a black cab driver. “It’s a stressful trade,” he says. “It’s made even more stressful by sudden roadworks, or ongoing protests by groups such as Palestine Action blocking roads. I can lose up to £300 a day in earnings when protests take place.”

Those still working as black cab drivers are now finding alternative methods to keep themselves afloat. O’Beirne works as a tour guide during slow periods. “I prefer doing out-of-town stuff now, because London just drives me mad,” he says. “Places like Tottenham Court Road, Mayfair, the City are just no-go areas. It’s a nightmare to navigate.

“Corporate gigs tend to pay better – I get a lot of American customers, who tend to be big tippers. But doing these gigs means you give up some freedom of setting your own hours, which is partly the beauty of doing this job.”

So, what could help London’s black cab drivers stay on the roads? For Cox, cutting, or at least reducing, some of the restrictions of LTNs on Hackney carriages would help with picking up and dropping off customers.

“If we really want to ease congestion, we could put a cap on private-hire drivers,” he adds. “They can operate without any restriction on the size of their fleet. I’ve heard of private-hire drivers getting licensed in Wolverhampton and then driving down here to get more work.”

Currently, private-hire vehicles vastly outstrip black cabs; TfL estimates more than 106,000 of these cabs are on the road.

Meanwhile, Steve Kenton, who has been a cabbie for 35 years, believes City Hall should listen more to black cab drivers. “We have some wonderful and intelligent people who choose to become black cab drivers,” he says. “We spend three to four years perfecting the Knowledge of London so we know the proper routes around the city. We attract individuals from every demographic. Our views and concerns deserve to be treated with more respect.”

“Fundamentally, if our wheels aren’t moving, we aren’t making money,” O’Beirne agrees. “We’re the only fully accessible transport network in London. Not everyone can ride a bike.”

data.title: Any company that adopts surge pricing deserves to get wiped out
data.description:
A sandwich at Pret might cost extra if it is a busy lunchtime. A pint might be another 50p at Wetherspoons if there is a crush at the bar, while the strawberries could double in price at Tesco a few hours before the Wimbledon final.

According to a report from the Bank of England this week, a third of companies are planning to experiment with surge pricing this year, charging more for their products at peak times.

In a free market, companies can adopt any pricing model they think works for them. And yet surge pricing is a terrible idea. Customers want clear, consistent and preferably good-value pricing – and businesses that forget that will deserve to be wiped out by their rivals very quickly.

We have all become far too familiar with surge pricing already. We already know that four tickets to a Greek island with Ryanair – on the first day of the school holidays – will cost us a lot more than the same flight would on a Tuesday night in February.

Likewise, we know that an Uber is going to cost us a lot more when it is pouring with rain, and concert tickets may well quadruple in price if everyone wants to book the same gig. But it could be about to become a lot more common.

The Bank of England says 31pc of companies plan to introduce what it politely termed “market responsive pricing tools” this year, up from 21pc 12 months ago. It gets worse. The supermarkets may soon have the technology to change prices minute by minute, and AI bots may even be able to analyse customer data so precisely that prices can be varied not just by time, but by person as well.

In other words, if you really need a giant packet of Kettle chips and a couple of beers before settling down to watch the football – and who doesn’t – then the shop can charge you extra for them.

We can all understand why companies are experimenting with new pricing models. Technology has made it far easier to adjust prices in real time. It started with the apps, because while an old-fashioned taxi meter charged a fixed price per mile, Uber simply found a price that matched supply and demand, and that was different according to the time and place.

But now it is possible to vary prices all the time, and for almost every business. Restaurants and bars now have menus that have to be downloaded onto your phone, instead of being expensively reprinted every time the price goes up, while even the supermarkets increasingly have electronic displays instead of old-fashioned price tags.

Surge pricing allows the seller to maximise their profits, charging what the customer will bear, instead of sticking to a fixed price when they could have got more. And, in fairness, it allows companies to compensate for one-off factors, such as the huge rise in energy prices we have witnessed over the last few weeks without selling at a loss, which would otherwise be very hard.

The trouble is, while it might make sense occasionally, it is a terrible idea once the majority of companies attempt it.

There are three big problems. First, it is exhausting for consumers. For most people, as soon as they know that the pricing is dynamic, there is a temptation to hunt around for the best deal. Wait for 10 minutes and the same product might cost 10pc less. It is time consuming, and turns every purchase into a huge chore.

Next, no one wants to feel ripped off. You might be willing to pay more for a product or service than the next person, perhaps because you have more money, or you really need it, but it is always frustrating to feel you have paid more than you had to.

Finally it will prove inflationary. It is hard to escape the suspicion that, while there are occasionally bargains, at least if you don’t mind flying to Corfu via Dusseldorf at three in the morning, on the whole prices only surge upwards. Indeed, it is not hard to understand why the Bank of England is getting worried. There used to be a certain stickiness to prices, because it was hard work to change all the labels.

But now it can be done instantaneously, and at zero cost. It makes it a lot harder to keep prices under control, or even to measure them accurately given that they might start changing at bewildering speed. The traditional inflation statistics will become meaningless if they no longer reflect the prices being charged on a day-to-day basis.

Surge pricing might make sense on a McKinsey powerpoint presentation, but out in the real world it will prove a catastrophe for companies that start using it more than they absolutely have to.

What customers want is fair, consistent prices. We will probably hear lots of calls for government inquiries, regulations and controls, such as the outcry over the cost of Oasis tickets last year. But what we really need to do is to make sure there is plenty of competition, and that, as customers, we avoid businesses that try to make their prices dynamic.

Companies can introduce surge pricing if they want to. It is a free market and everyone can compete anyway they want to. There may even be a few oddballs out there who might enjoy an AI bot analysing their consumer preferences and charging them the highest price it thinks it can get away with.

But it won’t work for most mainstream businesses – and any companies that try it deserve to get wiped out by rivals who charge a fair price, and then stick with it, regardless of the weather or the time of day.

data.title: Bank to quit Britain in protest against ‘unfair’ £750m car finance bill
data.description:
A bank has said it will quit the UK in protest after an “unfair” scheme that will see its car finance compensation payouts soar to £750m.

FirstRand, which owns roughly a tenth of the UK car finance sector through MotoNovo, has vowed to abandon its UK operations as result of the City watchdog’s mass redress scheme for mis-sold car loans.

In a notice to shareholders, the South African bank said it would leave its motor finance operations in Britain because it no longer has the “risk appetite” for the UK.

The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) finalised details of the scheme at the end of March, which FirstRand said were “disproportionate and unfair”.

The terms of the scheme mean FirstRand now expects its compensation bill to hit £750m, up from its previous estimate of £510m.

The firm’s announcement shows how the fallout from the car finance scandal is rocking the sector as millions of borrowers prepare to claw back compensation.

Around 12 million drivers will be entitled for an average payout of £829 under the FCA’s scheme, which will cost the car finance industry as much as £9.1bn.

This compensation is for customers who were sold car finance agreements in which dealers received a fee from lenders based on the interest rate charged on the loans.

The FCA, which banned these agreements in 2021, said these fees were rarely disclosed to customers and provided an incentive for dealers to charge higher interest rates than necessary.

Lenders including FirstRand had lobbied heavily for the compensation scheme to be watered down. The FCA did make some changes to the scheme following feedback from industry, but the South African group said that this was outweighed by other “problematic” amendments.

FirstRand’s higher payout estimate came as a result of the watchdog’s decision to broaden the scope of what it considered to be an “unfair” loan and changes to the amount of interest on unfair loans that a company would have to pay back to customers.

Back in October, the FCA had said it expected average payouts to total a lower sum of £700.

The South African bank said that it reserved its legal rights to challenge the scheme and that it believed that the FCA’s terms were “deeply flawed” and diverged “significantly and inappropriately” from a previous Supreme Court ruling.

The total cost for FirstRand would far exceed the £275m in profits that it has made on its motor finance activities in the UK over the past decade.

An FCA spokesman said: “Our scheme provides certainty and is the most cost efficient and orderly way to deal with liabilities that exist, no matter what. We welcome lenders, First Rand included, making financial provisions so they can deliver it.

“The market remains resilient – a record £41bn was lent on motor finance in 2025 and February’s new car sales were the highest in 22 years. With reforms now in place, we do not see further major redress events on the horizon.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Annabel’s and The Ivy sold to Arab sheikh for £1.4bn
data.description:
Richard Caring has struck a £1.4bn deal to sell his Ivy restaurant empire to an Abu Dhabi sheikh.

The restaurateur, dubbed the “King of Mayfair”, has sold a majority stake in his hospitality company to a group controlled by Sheik Tahnoun bin Zayed Al Nahyan, an Abu Dhabi royal known as the “spy sheikh”.

The deal, which values the company at more than £1.4bn, also includes the private members’ club Annabel’s and Mayfair staple Scott’s.

Mr Caring, 77, started his career in the fashion industry as a supplier to shops including Marks & Spencer before buying the original Ivy restaurant in 2005.

He expanded the brand from its original Covent Garden site – a popular haunt for actors – into a chain with dozens of Ivy cafes, brasseries and restaurants across the UK and Ireland.

It is understood that the tycoon will stay on as executive chairman of the group.

The deal, which was first reported by The Sunday Times, was struck with Diafa, an Abu Dhabi-based luxury and hospitality investor linked to Sheik Tahnoun’s International Holding Company (IHC).

Sheik Tahnoun is the son of the United Arab Emirates founder Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan and a leading figure in Abu Dhabi’s royal family. He is also the UAE’s intelligence chief, a position that has earned him the nickname the “spy sheikh”.

Diafa, which owns the Zuma and Roma restaurant brands, has reportedly brought in Ravi Thakran, a former top executive at French luxury giant LVMH, to lead the business.

The deal will also add the restaurants Sexy Fish and Noema to its portfolio, as well as Harry’s Bar, George and Mark’s Club.

The blockbuster deal will place control of some of London’s most high-profile restaurants in the hands of IHC, which is one of the most valuable companies on Abu Dhabi’s stock exchange. Its investments are spread across industries ranging from property to fisheries.

Mr Caring has long been seeking to sell his stake in The Ivy, with increasing fears that a deal would not materialise.

It marks a boost for Britain’s beleaguered hospitality industry, which is reeling from Labour’s National Insurance and minimum wage increases, which have pushed up labour costs sharply.

A spokesman for Mr Caring declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Factory bosses warn 25,000 jobs at risk from Reeves’s business rates blow
data.description:
Rachel Reeves’s business rates tax raid will cost Britain’s fragile manufacturing sector £1bn and put 25,000 jobs at risk, a trade group has warned.

Factories across the country will pay £939m more in business rates this year after tax changes took effect at the start of April, according to research by Make UK, which represents thousands of manufacturers.

The trade body warned this will increase pressure on the sector at a time when it already faces “existential threats” from surging energy and employment costs.

In a new analysis published on Tuesday, Make UK said many firms will be unable to raise prices given Britain’s weak economy, meaning they will look to cut jobs to cope with the near-£1bn bill.

Some 25,000 jobs are at risk of redundancy to counter the higher business rates, Make UK estimates.

Economists have already warned of rising unemployment as a result of Donald Trump’s war in Iran, which investment bank ING has estimated could cost 100,000 British jobs in the coming months as a result of rising energy prices for employers.

Verity Davidge, policy director at Make UK, said: “This increase couldn’t come at a worse possible time and is set to hammer one of the Government’s key strategic sectors, which is already facing existential threats from increased energy and employment costs, which are completely out of their control.

“For many companies right now, just to survive the burdens being imposed on them will be an achievement in itself.”

Most commercial properties – from offices to shops and warehouses – must pay business rates, which are typically charged on a premise’s “rateable value”. This is based on how much it could be rented out for.

Manufacturers warn that the sector faces a disproportionate hit from rising business rates, as they tend to occupy much bigger spaces than other companies.

As a result, two businesses with the same staff numbers and turnover can face big differences in their business rates bill depending on what industry they are in.

Ms Davidge said: “The current system of business rates is outdated and is a blunt instrument that leaves manufacturers paying disproportionately more than other sectors relative to their size.”

The Chancellor’s new rules dictate that large properties that could command more than £500,000 in rent annually face a higher tax rate, in a blow to factories.

The trade body also warned that factories are discouraged from investing in solar panels and other green ways to offset their energy bills, as it is likely to increase the rateable value of the property and thereby their business rates bill.

Britain’s job market is already suffering the highest rate of unemployment since the height of the pandemic.

The rise in business rates coincided with the minimum wage rising to £12.71 at the start of April, up by 50p.

Britain’s minimum wage is now one of the highest in the world, increasing by 43pc in the last five years – well ahead of inflation. 

Make UK is calling on the Government to find a better way to tax properties, such as linking the bill directly to turnover and sales so it is proportional to performance.

The trade body also wants businesses to get 12 months’ notice before new rates take effect.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Asda considers vending machines to fight shoplifting
data.description:
Asda is drawing up proposals to put perfume and batteries into vending machines to stop criminals stealing them from shelves as Britain grapples with a shoplifting epidemic.

The supermarket is holding talks about installing vending machines across its stores for products that are regularly targeted by criminals.

This includes potentially placing batteries, razor blades and perfumes into these machines, taking them off the shelves so that customers would have to pay before accessing them. These items tend to be at higher risk of being stolen as they are easier for criminal gangs to sell on the black market.

The discussions follow a trial of a vending machine in an Asda store in Ashton-under-Lyne, Greater Manchester, where it uses the technology to sell vapes and cigarettes.

The system involves customers choosing what they want on a screen and receiving a ticket that they take to tills to pay for. They then receive a second ticket which they can take to a vending machine that dispenses the product they want.

Speaking to staff at a meeting late last month, Asda bosses said they were looking at options for vending machines for “high-theft items”.

No decisions have been made yet on which items would be included in vending machines and a rollout is not believed to be imminent.

Supermarkets are racing to find ways to stop criminals from targeting their stores without putting staff at risk.

Marks & Spencer last weekend warned that crime was getting worse rather than better.

Writing in The Telegraph, Thinus Keeve, the company’s retail chief, said attacks were becoming “more brazen, more organised and more aggressive” across Britain’s high streets.

He said recent incidents included gangs forcing open locked cabinets and stripping shelves, as well as staff being headbutted and having ammonia thrown in their face.

The latest crime report compiled by the British Retail Consortium (BRC) found that there were 5.5 million recorded shoplifting incidents last year, costing retailers nearly £400m. However, the BRC said the total number of incidents was likely to be higher as supermarkets were not always recording or aware of attacks.

M&S executives recently wrote to both the Mayor of London and the Home Secretary to demand they do more to tackle shoplifting and crime.

Mobs of youths have ran riot in Clapham, south London, over the past month, targeting an M&S store and lighting fires.

Supermarkets have spent more than £5bn in the last five years to improve security measures, investing in more CCTV and security staff at doorways.

However, retailers are becoming increasingly concerned about staff getting involved to prevent attacks.

It emerged last week that Waitrose had dismissed an employee after he attempted to stop a shoplifter stealing a bag of Easter eggs. Waitrose said at the time that the “correct process is being followed, which includes a standard appeals procedure”.

Other British supermarkets have also trialled vending-machine technology to address the issue, including Morrisons and Sainsbury’s.

It is more widely used across the rest of Europe – particularly in Norway and Sweden – to stock products including medication and phone cards.

A spokesman for Asda said: “We continually explore new ideas which may help to improve our customers’ and colleagues’ experiences whilst in our stores. However, there are no immediate plans to install new vending machines for any products in our stores whilst a trial in our Ashton store is ongoing.”
