data.title: Ovo Energy sues TalkTalk over botched takeover
data.description:
Ovo Energy has sued troubled broadband giant TalkTalk over a botched deal to buy up customers.

The energy company has launched a legal claim in a dispute about the sale of broadband customers to TalkTalk, as Ovo scrambles to secure a sale.

Ovo sold SSE Energy Services’s phone and broadband business in 2022, which saw 135,000 customers transferred to TalkTalk.

The company had acquired the telecoms business following its takeover of SSE Energy Services, a gas and electricity provider, two years previously.

To complete the deal, TalkTalk paid an initial sum upfront and agreed to make further contributions once certain milestones were met.

However, the deal soured after swathes of customers left TalkTalk, rendering the takeover largely pointless. TalkTalk has since refused to pay the outstanding sum to Ovo, prompting the company to launch a legal claim.

The lawsuit underscores efforts by Ovo to settle its affairs as bankers scramble to complete a sale.

Ovo, which is Britain’s fourth-largest gas and electricity supplier with four million customers, has been attempting to raise £300m in fresh investment for months in an effort to shore up its finances.

The company is facing uncertainty over its future after failing to meet financial stress tests introduced by regulator Ofgem in the wake of the 2022 energy crisis, which caused dozens of suppliers to collapse.

Bankers at Rothschild have been appointed to oversee the fundraising process, with a sale of the business also under consideration. It is thought that any deal will not include Ovo’s technology arm Kaluza.

Ovo has also begun cutting tens of millions of pounds in costs under a new business plan aimed at securing its survival.

Despite warnings about its ability to stay afloat and posting losses of £135m in 2024, Ovo still paid out £27m that year to a company owned by Stephen Fitzpatrick, its founder.

Mr Fitzpatrick, who founded Ovo in 2009, has extracted tens of millions of pounds through a licensing deal to use the brand. The energy company last year bought the brand outright in a £150m deal.

Mr Fitzpatrick’s other ventures include Kensington Roof Gardens, a private members’ club in west London once owned by Sir Richard Branson.

The legal claim will create a problem for TalkTalk, which is struggling under its £1.4bn debt burden.

Surging interest costs have pushed the company to heavy losses and caused it to fall behind on payments to suppliers including Openreach, BT’s network division.

The troubles have prompted shareholders, including Ares Management and Sir Charles Dunstone, the executive chairman of the broadband giant, to provide repeated cash injections to keep the company afloat. The latest £115m lifeline in March marked the third such deal since the end of 2024.

TalkTalk is also seeking a buyer for part or whole of the business. Larger rival Virgin Media O2 had been considering a deal for its consumer division but has now walked away.

Ovo and TalkTalk declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Shadow banking tycoon’s company wrongly claimed FCA approval
data.description:
A company tied to the collapsed shadow bank Market Financial Solutions (MFS) misled investors by wrongly claiming it had regulatory approval.

Creative Market Finance, formerly owned by Paresh Raja, the founder of MFS, wrongly claimed for two years that it had a licence from the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA).

Between 2016 and 2018, the company advertised the seal of approval prominently on its website, although it is understood that it was unlicensed throughout this period.

The licence advertised was for consumer credit activities, although Creative Market Finance’s (CMF) business was instead based on raising money from individuals and investing.

These activities would have required separate permissions from the FCA that the company did not have.

At the time of the false advertising, CMF described itself as a “sister company” to MFS, forming part of Mr Raja’s crop of so-called bridging lenders in the UK.

MFS provided short-term, property-backed loans to borrowers who may not have qualified for traditional bank financing and often charged higher interest rates.

The revelations will raise questions about whether regulators missed early red flags within Mr Raja’s “shadow banking” empire.

The entrepreneur has been hit with a worldwide asset-freezing order after MFS collapsed in February amid allegations of a “very serious” fraud.

Mr Raja first bought a 50pc stake in CMF in 2016, co-owning the business with Zaheed Nizar, a former banker at Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley.

Mr Nizar was banned for nine years in 2024 from serving as a company director because of a Covid loan fraud concerning a separate hotel business he owned.

It is understood that CMF had previously obtained FCA approval, but the licence had expired once Mr Raja had taken over the business.

At the time, the company claimed its funds were managed with the “highest level of security and professionalism”.

It promised the “utmost secure and safest form of lending” and “guaranteed income for each of our investors”.

Both Mr Nizar and Mr Raja appeared intent on raising money from individuals in the British Asian community, featuring alongside each other in an interview in Asian Voice in 2015, which directed prospective investors to MFS’s website.

CMF was a brief part of Mr Raja’s shadow banking, although MFS later became the main part of his business.

The company, which described itself as a specialist provider of buy-to-let mortgage lending and bridging finance, at one point boasted of a loan book of £2.5bn.

Fallout with creditors

However, it collapsed into administration earlier this year amid allegations of fraud.

Founded and owned by Mr Raja, MFS did not take deposits but was instead funded by borrowing from banks and other financial firms.

This included large borrowings from institutional investors such as Barclays and Jefferies, both of which are now facing steep losses.

The fallout has led to creditors accusing Mr Raja of running a network of fake companies to steal money from them.

In a claim filed in the High Court, the creditors warned that lending to connected borrowers, as well as the “double-pledging” of collateral to secure multiple loans, triggered a £1.3bn hole in MFS’s accounts.

Representatives for Mr Raja have called the allegations from creditors “materially incorrect” and said there had been no intention to defraud.

They have also claimed Mr Raja was not the beneficiary of any shortfall, if there was one.

They declined to comment on questions related to CMF. Mr Nizar did not respond to a request to comment.

The FCA declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The $60bn deal that spells the end of rock ’n’ roll
data.description:
Bill Ackman is an unlikely music mogul.

A Donald Trump backer and vocal supporter of Israel, Ackman’s successful investments to date have been largely focused on tech giants such as Uber and Meta, as well as the unglamorous world of mortgage-backed securities.

Yet Ackman is attempting to pull off an audacious swoop for Universal, the world’s largest record label that is home to best-selling artists including Taylor Swift and Harry Styles.

Ackman, who already holds a 4.5pc stake in Universal through his company Pershing Square, has tabled a $64bn (£48bn) offer to take control of the music giant and shift its stock market listing from Amsterdam to New York.

For the hedge fund tycoon, the move is a way of unlocking value from a company he argues is unloved by the market.

More widely, though, it threatens to accelerate a move by major record labels away from new music and towards the safety of trusted old catalogues, as the unstoppable rise of streaming sounds the death knell for the bygone days of rock ’n’ roll.

Ackman’s complex proposal, made public this week, would see Universal reversed into Pershing’s blank-cheque company and listed on the New York Stock Exchange at a value of around $64bn.

Pershing, which would increase its stake to almost 12pc, has promised to return €9.4bn (£8bn) to shareholders, as well as handing up to €750m to artists by selling Universal’s 3pc stake in Spotify, which is worth around €2.7bn.

Ackman, who also set out plans to appoint Michael Ovitz, a veteran Hollywood agent, as chairman, has long been agitating for change at Universal, whose share price has dropped by more than 30pc over the last year.

He argues that it is undervalued compared with its rivals and should tap into deeper pools of capital on the US market.

Ultimately, the success of the deal will largely depend on the Bolloré family. The French industrial dynasty is the largest shareholder through both a personal stake and its company Vivendi, which owned Universal prior to its spin-off and stock market listing in 2021.

The family’s commitment to Universal has been in doubt since its representative stood down from the company’s board last summer and as patriarch Vincent Bolloré prepares to stand trial on allegations of corruption in Africa, which he denies.

Ackman claims that the Bolloré family assured him the proposal was “music to our ears”, though he admitted to investors that “without Bolloré we don’t have a transaction”. Neither Bolloré nor Vivendi has commented publicly on the proposal.

Another significant factor is Sir Lucian Grainge, the British executive who runs Universal and is widely considered the most powerful person in music.

Ackman laid out his proposals to Sir Lucian at a recent dinner at Ovitz’s art-filled Beverly Hills mansion and said the label boss “encouraged us to send it in”.

Universal has not commented beyond saying it will review Pershing’s proposals. If Sir Lucian embraces the deal, however, it could have something to do with how much he stands to make.

The mogul, who grew up in north London, previously faced an investor backlash about his high pay packets, including pocketing a $140m bonus from Universal’s stock market float.

A recent contract extension included stock-linked bonuses of up to $100m, which would vest at three hurdles when Universal’s share price reaches €26.50, €30 and €38.

At its current price of €19, these hurdles remain a way off. But Ackman’s deal, which values Universal at €30.40, would trigger the first two.

Pershing also said it would implement a new employment contract and compensation arrangement for the Universal boss, though the details of this are not yet clear.

Sir Lucian’s doyen status embodies Britain’s outsized role in the global music market, with homegrown artists achieving success that far exceeds this island’s size. The UK recorded music market exceeded £1.5bn in revenue for the first time last year thanks to a new generation of artists including Olivia Dean and Lola Young.

But this dominance is slipping. No British artists made it into the top 10 for the best-selling singles or albums globally in 2024 – the first time this has happened in more than two decades, according to figures from the IFPI.

The fact that Universal is now poised to shift to New York, rather than London, is another reminder of that declining status. When Universal bought EMI for £1.2bn in 2011, it left Britain without one of the record labels known collectively as the “majors”.

The others are Warner Music, controlled by Ukraine-born billionaire Sir Leonard Blavatnik, and Sony Music, a US subsidiary of the Japanese conglomerate.

‘There are some real crown jewels here’

Ackman’s swoop on Universal now raises the prospect of a bidding war, with private equity firms including KKR mooted as potential suitors.

It comes as debt-fuelled dealmaking in the media sector reaches fever pitch following the $55bn takeover of gaming group Electronic Arts last year and Paramount’s recent knockout $111bn bid for Warner Bros.

Hartwig Masuch, the former chief executive of German label giant BMG, where he focused on the building of and cashing in on catalogue, said Universal could easily be worth more than Ackman’s opening bid and even justify an $80bn price tag.

He said: “There are some real crown jewels here that are of huge interest to investors like insurance companies and sovereign wealth.

“The Beatles’ recordings on their own could be a listed company. But even some of the second-tier stuff is amazing property – think of Tears for Fears, for instance.”

The growing interest in Universal’s catalogue could also trigger takeover interest in arch-rival Warner Music.

More widely, however, the role of the majors is now in doubt. The rise of streaming has made virtually all recorded music in history readily available and introduced new generations to old hits. Unlike in the analogue era, recordings no longer have a limited shelf life and novelty is no longer the name of the game.

This is borne out in listening habits. Just 27pc of US streaming consumption in 2024 came from new releases, compared with 45pc a decade ago, according to figures cited by Robert Kyncl, the Warner Music boss.

This has been accelerated in recent years as platforms such as Spotify shift their focus away from acquiring new subscribers to retaining existing ones, making familiar songs and artists more prominent than new or experimental ones.

“The whole playback environment becomes much safer, much more focus on what people call ‘lean-back’ listening,” says Patrick Clifton, the executive director at indie-label think-tank Orca.

Savvy artists and investors have cottoned on. As the value of publishing and recording back catalogues has surged, the likes of Pink Floyd, Bob Dylan, Bruce Springsteen and Justin Bieber have lined up to sell their publishing rights in deals worth hundreds of millions of dollars. In 2024, Queen agreed to sell its song catalogue to Sony for a record-breaking £1bn.

A string of enterprising new businesses has cropped up specialising in investing in back catalogues. Best known among them was Hipgnosis, a fund launched by Merck Mercuriadis, a former manager of Beyoncé and Elton John. 

Hipgnosis was quick to pounce on the value of song catalogues but ultimately overpaid for rights. It was acquired by Blackstone for $1.6bn in 2024 as private equity firms also sought to get in on the act.

It is a trend that has proved controversial in the industry, triggering accusations that opportunistic buyers are profiting from the success built by labels and turning music from culture to commodity.

“It’s a bit disappointing to see a focus on music as this new asset class, which is the language that increasingly we hear,” says Clifton.

Nevertheless, the major labels are the undisputed winners of this phenomenon thanks to their deep recording catalogues stretching back decades.

Keeping the lights on

In a jargon-heavy letter to investors last month, Warner Music’s Kyncl promised to deliver “accelerated growth through margin-accretive acquisitions of high-quality catalogues”.

The same is true of Universal. “It’s Universal’s catalogue that keeps the lights on,” says Sir Robin Millar, the veteran music producer known as the man behind Sade. “You use the surplus to invest in new acts.”

He adds: “If I was sitting on the Universal board, I’d be the one putting my hand up saying if we’ve got surplus, buy more catalogue.”

As catalogue has grown in importance, doubt has been cast over the less predictable side of the talent-spotting business known as A&R [artists and repertoire]. Labels have historically adopted a high-risk strategy that involves making bets on emerging artists in the hope that, while most will fail, one will break through with significant success.

But an increasing consumption of back catalogues and the wider atomisation of listening as a result of streaming mean it is harder for labels to make a return on their investments.

“The indies try promoting unknown acts from scratch and it appears to be nigh on impossible,” says Sir Robin.

“What you’re waiting for therefore is artists who’ve built their own significant social media following and streaming following. They become very valuable commodities because the major record labels feel all the spadework is done.”

For hard-headed money men such as Ackman, the A&R model has a different financial profile, which is likely to dilute the company’s overall value.

As a result, labels are evolving from companies that discover artists and nurture them throughout their careers to simple rights-holders trading catalogues as commodities.

Annabella Coldrick, the chief executive of the Music Managers Forum, says: “Major labels still play an important role in the music ecosystem, particularly regarding investment and marketing, but their focus has shifted considerably in the streaming era as the value of catalogue has skyrocketed.”

Ackman will be well aware of this evolution. In a presentation to investors, Pershing outlined plans to load the label giant with debt to fund acquisitions, accelerating a series of acquisitions that most recently included a controversial $775m deal to buy indie label Downtown Music.

Pershing also shrugged off concerns about the impact of AI in the industry, arguing that this would only solidify the importance of trusted labels while creating opportunities to generate new revenues from Universal’s catalogue.

Ackman’s evident interest in Universal’s catalogue will raise questions about whether the hedge fund boss would ultimately seek to hive off the A&R side of the business altogether.

At the same time, his plans to sell Universal’s stake in Spotify would further sever the company’s control over the distribution of music it makes.

Universal insiders argue the label is still the biggest investor in new music and point out its artists have won the breakthrough prize at the Brit Awards for the last two years. But as the industry shifts its focus from new music to trusty classics, the traditional role of the record label is undoubtedly waning. And with it, some critics argue, culture.

For many in the industry, these changes in the record-label model are not merely a commercial evolution but represents a worrying trend for music and culture. Ackman’s plans for Universal, critics say, are yet another demonstration of that.

“It doesn’t feel like there’s a lot of focus on music or culture,” says Gee Davy, the chief executive of the Association of Independent Music, which represents indie labels.

“It feels very shareholder-driven, very share [price] driven and to some extent technology-driven. When you’re looking primarily towards shareholders, you’re not going to be able to spend as much time focusing on culture or artists.”

Optimistic observers suggest that any move by Universal away from A&R would present an opportunity for Britain to re-establish its credentials as a crucible of talent, building on the success of indie labels such as Beggars and Domino.

For Sir Robin, it’s a question of how modern record companies hope to discover the next big thing. The world of social media, after all, is not very rock ’n’ roll.

“I have a lot of confidence that there’ll be more Olivia Deans. What I have much more concern about is [that] you’re relying on artists who have proven themselves to be internet geniuses and TikTok dancers as the people that will get the big record deals,” he says.

“The people I think that changed the thinking of a generation are people that you wouldn’t get into a car with if they were driving. They’re poets, they’re dreamers ... Those are the people that matter.”

A Pershing Square spokesman said: “Sir Lucian Grainge and management have done a superb job nurturing and building a world-class artist roster.

“We are fully committed to ensuring that UMG will have whatever resources are necessary to continue to be the leading music company in the world.”

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.

data.title: Town centres face losing their car parks after operator’s collapse
data.description:
Scores of car parks across Britain face closure following the sudden collapse of National Car Parks (NCP), in a development that threatens to deal a hammer blow to town centres.

Administrators for NCP, the country’s biggest operator, are threatening to abandon sites if landlords refuse to accept drastically lower rents.

Up to 20 car parks have already been shut and councils are now braced for dozens more closures across NCP’s remaining 318 sites.

Closures threaten not only to leave landlords out of pocket but also deal a blow to councils, which risk missing out on millions of pounds in business rates. An unoccupied building owned by a company in administration is typically exempt from business rates.

The closure of NCP car parks would also be a blow for town centres. Many are the main sites for people visiting shopping centres, meaning visitor numbers could suffer.

Insolvency experts at PwC and CBRE are currently scrambling to safeguard NCP’s future after it collapsed into administration this month.

This first involved stripping out loss-making sites across the company’s estate before then demanding lower rents from its landlords. Administrators are now seeking a buyer for the business.

However, the process has already been frustrated by tensions with landlords who are furious at administrators’ threats to close sites if terms cannot be agreed. In some cases, landlords are being asked to reduce rents by more than 50pc.

As well as seeking significantly cheaper rates, administrators have also demanded a four-week rent waiver to help put the company on a stable footing.

Landlords have been given just days to agree to new terms on sites occupied by NCP, increasing the likelihood of widespread closures.

One landlord said they were given 24 hours’ notice of the new offer and told to accept or face immediate closure.

“We view these last-minute bullying tactics as the lowest form of negotiation and are not prepared to do business with people who behave in this manner,” they said.

City lawyers are already in contact with landlords in the event of potential legal action, with some considering whether to take sites back from NCP.

It is understood that the sites already closed have effectively been abandoned, including those in Birmingham, Leicester, London and Luton.

One landlord said they were not even told the site had been closed.

It has sparked alarm among city councils over fears that empty car parks will become a magnet for anti-social behaviour.

Some local officials are already emailing landlords to ask what security measures will be put in place to keep sites safe.

The importance of car parks to local economies has also prompted speculation that some councils may have to foot the bill for reopening sites to support businesses. Such a move would increase pressure on already cash-strapped local authorities.

The developments will trigger scrutiny of NCP’s former Japanese owner, Park24. The private equity firm blamed a combination of Covid, high rents and elevated energy costs for NCP’s downfall, while PwC said the company came unstuck as “significant rent payments” fell due.

However, others have pointed to spiralling borrowings across the business, which rose to £350m by the end of 2025. Some City sources said these debts were largely to blame for the company’s collapse.

NCP charged high prices and had limited overheads, suggesting it should have been financially stable without debt. In central London, NCP charges motorists as much as £60 a day for parking.

Following their appointment, PwC said the business had “deteriorated over a number of years post Covid-19 as demand for parking has not recovered to historic levels, particularly across city-centre and commuter locations”.

Administrators said last night they were still working hard to sell some or all of the business, which was founded in 1931 by Colonel Frederick Lucas before merging with rival Central Car Parks in 1959.

A spokesman for the administrators said: “A small number of commercially unviable sites have closed this week and while no other closures are currently planned, the administrators are engaging with key stakeholders, including landlords of some sites, to reach agreements that will allow for their continued operation.”

data.title: Every shop seems to want my email address and it’s killing customer service
data.description:
When a cashier asks me in a shop: “Do you have a loyalty card?” I have a small but enjoyable ritual of explaining: “No, I am very disloyal.” If I’m feeling extra-capricious I may add: “In fact, I’m planning to cheat on you with that store over there.”

I feel strongly that my patronage should be dependent on that day’s service, not a matter of flexing a bit of plastic in return for points and desultory offers. We all know that this supposed “fidelity” has absolutely zero to do with your devotion to a particular emporium, but is about them capturing your data, analysing your shopping habits and overflowing your inbox with unwanted emails.

Despite my strenuous efforts to evade consumer Big Brother, they are forever finding devious new ways of extracting your personal details. I bought a T-shirt for my son’s 18th birthday in a small, vogueish store and was confounded when they said they didn’t print receipts, but would email it to me. “What if I don’t want you to have my email?” I asked. The hurt sales assistant said that they had ditched paper to be more sustainable and reduce their carbon footprint – leaving the unspoken accusation in the air: “Do you want to hurt the planet?”

Big stores such as Nike have been using this ploy on the shop floor for ages – though they will print a receipt if you ask for one – but it makes my blood boil when a simple request to print out proof of your expenditure feels like a war crime. 

A friend told me that Oliver Bonas, the UK-based homeware and fashion chain, is also walking down the no-receipt route. She dashed out to buy a present in her lunch hour, only to find a swift purchase became an extended session of filling in multiple details on an iPad, while other customers queued behind her. This seems particularly egregious in a store that’s known for having outlets in stations, where speed is the whole blooming point of the exercise.

This insistence on sworn allegiance can feel highly counterproductive. I am a bona fide devotee of Hotel Chocolat’s wares, feeling they bridge the gap between deluxe cacao brands like Rococo and pretend-posh ones like Lily O’Brien’s in superb fashion. But they’re always trying to browbeat you into flourishing a loyalty card, even though the only major benefit is 15 per cent off hot chocolate and ice creams in their stores (I don’t want to sit in their branches being fattened up like Augustus Gloop), and being entered into a “draw” which you’re even less likely to win than that Omaze house.

The fact is, customer satisfaction comes a sorry second to extorting personal information and finding ways to bind us to their brand. In fact, I wouldn’t be at all surprised if I looked at a store’s small print and found that I’d signed away my soul, my house and my firstborn son – which is obviously a fair exchange for being first in the queue for the Father’s Day offer.

Why can’t shopping return to being a simple and even pleasurable pastime, where customers gradually establish a true bond with the proprietor or manager? I first got to know three of my good friends in Cambridge when I started going into their shops. One sold frocks, another traded shoes, while the third, as you might expect, was a wine merchant. 

All of them had the unique ability to understand your personal needs and make you feel valued. As a regular, you would often find an unexpected and generous discount applied to your bill. They only got my email, phone number and personal secrets when we became mates. Trust should be earned, not stored up in cyberspace.

data.title: The investment gems hidden in Britain’s net zero drive
data.description:
Given the events in the Gulf, one might suppose that the UK’s diverse renewable energy sector, centred on alternative investment trusts, would be surging ahead.

Sadly, you’d be mistaken. To be fair, some funds have recorded meagre gains in the last month, and overall, the sector has avoided the significant losses seen in the wider market.

However, the sector overall has averaged a gain of just 1pc so far this year and an average decline of 0.4pc since the war began.

Politically inspired critics of renewable energy will no doubt rush to judge.

But in reality, a rather long list of parochial, very specific issues has undermined the sector, helping push the three-year return to a queasy 28pc loss.

I would argue that there is a distinct possibility that we may have seen the worst, with a handful of funds offering decent income opportunities for investors, but it’s also true that the challenges facing the space might worsen in the short term.

What went wrong? On paper, the income-driven, renewables fund sector – worth about £10bn in gross assets and the envy of much of Europe – should be very boring and reliable.

It should churn out a predictable income from wind and solar assets backed by government schemes such as Renewable Obligation Certificates (ROCs) and Feed-in tariffs (FIT).

It’s true that fixed revenues account for something between 60pc and 80pc of the revenues of the 10 funds that dominate the space, with ROCs a chunky proportion of that income.

But it’s also true that the variable price of energy and the amount of energy produced do have an impact, and both have been trending down – largely.

Until Iran, the various forward power price curves used in valuation models had also been trending down, which has indirectly fed through to a likely lower Ofgem price cap for UK consumers in the next quarter.

But the killer blow was government policy. Last year, the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero launched a consultation on changing the basis of payments (ROCs and FITs), which resulted in a switch from the higher RPI rate of inflation to CPI from April 2026.

This move was not as bad as many in the industry feared – there was an even worse option – but the whole process undermined investor confidence mightily.

And talking of investors, they were also getting increasingly worried about the cost of refinancing the debt used in these structures, an issue that impacts many other sectors including real estate.

The average cost of debt across most renewable funds is between 3.6pc and 4.6pc per annum. A few funds have meaningful amounts of debt and frequently expensive revolving credit facilities, due to be rescheduled in the next few years.

Not unsurprisingly, investors put two and two together and worried that the rising cost of capital might threaten the generous dividends.

That in turn exposed a slightly hidden secret: where’s the growth coming from? On paper, the underlying energy assets seem like cleverly engineered evergreen cash machines, just churning out subsidy-backed income, but the assets have a fixed life, and in most cases, a fixed life of contracts.

What happens when those contracts finish, and the assets need replacing? Where’s the cash going to come from to renew the asset base if you’re paying it all out as dividend income?

Just to make matters worse, worries about the sustainability of dividend income intensified during the last few months just as two-year and 10-year gilt yields rose. These higher yields are a double whammy.

First, they increase the risk-free rates built into the valuation models, and secondly, they make the dividends paid out by many funds less attractive, especially if you can get, say, 5pc for lending to the Government long term and risk-free (although fans of the sector might argue that gilt yields are fixed whereas many fund dividends might grow with inflation).

Add it all up, and you have the perfect storm in recent months for a sector that has produced average returns of 7pc per annum since 2013, including 6.3pc from income. Discounts have widened sharply and now run at around 35pc for the broad sector average, according to fund analysts at JP Morgan, which is very high by historical standards.

A cynic might at this point argue that all these trends could get worse. Discounts could widen as dividends are cut. There have also been some unforced management errors regarding strategic reviews in the sector, undermining investor confidence in many fund boards.

The optimist, and I’m a cautious one, thinks that most of the bad news is already in the price, especially if you select your fund carefully. 

The policy regime is unlikely to change until the next general election, and I’m one of the few optimists who think UK gilt rates might possibly have overshot on the way up. Who knows? The Iran crisis might even help with higher energy prices in the medium term.

Nevertheless, even I’d still be cautious at this stage and opt for funds that have more conservative balance sheets and assets at scale in the right places.

Two names jump into my mind: one from a related but distinct sector, Greencoat UK Wind (UKW), and the other, a more diversified infrastructure fund, International Public Partnerships (INPP).

The first, Greencoat UK Wind, is the biggest fund in the space and boasts a progressive dividend policy backed by £1bn of free cash flow over the next five years. 

Power prices could drop to as little as £30 per megawatt hour before the dividend is uncovered, and the 42.5pc gearing seems manageable, although there is a chunk of debt coming up for refinancing in 2027.

Its discount is the lowest in the sector at around 29pc according to JP Morgan, and its dividend is an attractive 10.8pc.

JP Morgan analysts recently said: “We think UKW offers a reasonable hedge for investors concerned about the possibility of higher power prices and inflation.” I’d agree.

By contrast, INPP is a broadly diversified infrastructure investor that has given careful thought to the growth problem. It’s invested heavily in two crucial pieces of energy infrastructure: new transmission cables, which are essential for handling intermittent and geographically uneven output, and nuclear power.

The transmission assets are worth around a fifth of the portfolio, while the nuclear assets consist of a £254m commitment to Sizewell C.

Last week’s robust trading numbers showed a diversified £2.2bn fund that has delivered just under 100pc NAV growth in the last 10 years, on a well-backed dividend of 6.8pc.

Regardless of whether you are a net zero cynic, more intermittent renewable energy is going to surge into the system during the next few years and energy prices will remain high, which should benefit Greencoat UK Wind – which has lots of valuable onshore wind assets.

That means more transmission spending and more base-load nuclear, which should count in INPP’s favour.

data.title: North Sea gas ‘saves Britain billions a year’
data.description:
North Sea natural gas has been found to have saved Britain billions last year, increasing pressure on the Government to boost production during the Iran war.

Tapping into its own resources of offshore gas instead of paying for imported liquefied natural gas (LNG) shipments saved the UK about £2.5bn last year, according to analysis from investment bank Stifel.

The bank said savings on UK-produced gas would be “substantially higher” this year because prices have surged during the Middle East conflict.

Gas prices have soared by 56pc since the start of the war as the threat of strikes has deterred tankers from passing through the Strait of Hormuz, where a fifth of global exports usually pass.

Meanwhile, missile damage at Qatar’s Ras Laffan plant, which produces a fifth of the world’s LNG, is expected to take three to five years to repair.

Stifel’s analysis will pile further pressure on Ed Miliband, who has banned new exploration in British waters and has so far rejected calls to extract more fossil fuels from the North Sea because of his net zero push.

The Energy Secretary has claimed that awarding new licences for North Sea drilling would do little to lower prices.

However, Chris Wheaton, an analyst at Stifel, said it was astonishing that ministers were not ramping up production of North Sea gas, asking: “Doesn’t the Government realise there’s a war on?”

“Unsurprisingly – and we are frankly astonished that this has to be stated – the UK’s own North Sea gas is cheaper than imported LNG, and we estimate this saved the UK £2.5bn in 2025 alone,” he said. “With the increase in global LNG prices due to the Persian Gulf conflict, we expect that amount will be substantially higher in 2026.”

The North Sea is still the UK’s biggest gas source, accounting for around 45pc of its supplies. The other 35pc comes from piped gas from Norway, while 20pc is LNG imports.

‘Maximise production’

The Climate Change Commission has said Britain will still need oil and gas beyond 2050.

According to Stifel, this imported LNG has been, on average, 18p per therm more expensive than North Sea gas over the past few years, with this gap helping Britain save about £2.5bn in 2025.

“To minimise the cost of energy for the UK, the UK needs to maximise its own gas production,” Mr Wheaton added.

The Iran war has increased pressure on Mr Miliband to grant new licences for the Rosebank oil field and the Jackdaw gas field in the North Sea.

His opposition has prompted a growing backbench rebellion among Labour MPs, who are calling for a rethink of his net zero agenda.

Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, opened up a rift with the Energy Secretary last week, saying she was “very happy” to support drilling, which she said created “jobs and tax revenue”.

Manufacturing bosses and unions have also warned that factories will struggle to survive more cost pressures.

Stifel urged the Energy Secretary to reform the windfall tax on oil and gas producers, which it said was “taxing the UK’s own gas production to destruction”.

Mr Wheaton said this was “a decision which makes no sense to us whatsoever, as it increases the amount of expensive LNG imports”.

Offshore Energies UK has predicted the so-called Energy Profits Levy will see UK oil and gas output plunge by 40pc by the end of the decade.

Energy prices have moved violently during the past month with the onset of the Iran war. Brent crude oil, the international benchmark, rose to $111 a barrel on Monday before paring gains to $108.

Traders are watching for signs of easing tensions between the US and Iran after reports that both sides are working towards a ceasefire.

New analysis by Kpler, an energy research group, published on Monday, suggested oil prices would have to rise to between $160 and $170 to balance supply.

This would help cut demand by around six million barrels a day – global demand is around 104 million barrels a day – and reduce the risk of shortages.

data.title: The affordable homes rule fuelling Britain’s housing crisis
data.description:
It may have been designed with the best of intentions, but a decades-old policy to boost social housing has been blamed for strangling Britain’s property market.

Housebuilders are required by the Government to contribute affordable homes to secure planning consent for new projects.

Such homes are built for lower-income families and priced below local market rates, meaning less profit for the housebuilder. 

Some view the rule, called section 106  of the Town and Country Planning Act, as a vital safety net to offer low-income households a route into the property market. Others fear it is killing off housebuilding across Britain when it is needed most.

According to the most pessimistic estimates, affordable housing requirements have delayed more than 45,000 homes across the country.

Meanwhile, the Adam Smith Institute, a think tank, estimates that section 106 has cost builders £1.3bn in the past year alone, based on the grants they are losing out on and the cost of building the homes.

That is the equivalent of £13,000 for every open-market home built by a private developer.

Andrew Dawber, a founding partner of social housing developer Civitas Investment Management and a senior fellow at the think tank, says the system has become a brake on development of all kinds.

“Very few houses are getting started, of all descriptions: open market houses, affordable houses,” he adds.

Scrutiny of social housing delivery comes at a crucial time for the country as Labour appears increasingly unlikely to deliver on its pledge to build 1.5 million homes by 2030.

Despite this, the Government has so far held firm on retaining section 106.

A spokesman for the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government said: “We need more affordable homes and section 106 agreements help to get spades in the ground on housing developments, delivering more than 20,000 new affordable homes last year.

“We are introducing reforms to improve the section 106 system so it is simpler and more transparent and we can get on and build the homes and infrastructure this country desperately needs.”

Its stance has prompted a renewed dispute over whether the policy is doing more harm than good.

Sir James Cleverly, the shadow housing secretary, says: “Excessive section 106 requirements, as we have seen in London under Labour, make development unviable.

“Angela Rayner’s legacy means similar demands are being imposed across England. It all means less regeneration and less affordable housing as nothing ends up being built.”

When councils approve new private housing, it is usually granted on condition of a section 106 deal, requiring builders to provide a certain number of affordable homes on their developments. These are then typically sold to housing associations to operate.

However, small housebuilders in particular are struggling to get affordable homes built because they can’t find housing associations willing to take them on.

While housing associations are non-profit organisations, their finances are under pressure from capped social rents, limited government grants and rising maintenance costs.

One developer, who does not wish to be named, tells The Telegraph that his business has secured permission to build homes in the South West, a quarter of them affordable. The builder has approached 25 housing associations but has not received any responses.

This has left the whole site on hold despite years of pouring money into the planning process, leaving tradesmen without work and potential buyers in limbo.

James Hodgkinson, a researcher at the Adam Smith Institute, says: “It’s a system that paralyses development and critically, nobody wins.”

The boss of another medium-sized housebuilder, who does not wish to be named, describes how his team has had to start several sites without a housing association on board, posing a significant financial threat.

“We are getting extremely low offers when we are able to secure a housing association offer,” he says. “This is further denting margins and putting some developments in a loss-making position.

He claims this, alongside tax rises and growing policy costs, is pushing smaller housebuilders to “the verge of collapse”.

A failing model

Introduced in 1990 in the final year of Margaret Thatcher’s administration, section 106 rules evolved over the years and were significantly expanded by Tony Blair’s government.

At that point, section 106 was viewed as an attractive way to compel the private sector to put more cash into affordable homes.

By the time the Conservative-Liberal Democrat coalition came into power in 2010, the rule had become firmly embedded in the planning process.

What had once accounted for under 10pc of affordable housing delivery had grown to represent nearly half of all affordable homes built in the UK by the early 2020s, says Dawber.

The shift represented a fundamental change in housing policy, moving from direct investment to a system that relies more heavily on the health of the private market.

But that dependence, critics argue, has become the problem. With the open market buckling under mounting taxes, high interest rates and soaring build costs in recent years, section 106 is also suffering.

Government data published in November show that in 2024-25, 36pc of all new affordable homes delivered in England were bankrolled by section 106, down from 45pc in the previous year and a peak of 51pc in 2019-20.

This marked its lowest share since 2014-15.

How to fix it

The Adam Smith Institute believes the rule should be scrapped and replaced with grant funding through Homes England, the Government’s central housing agency.

“Section 106 is a lever that could be pulled quite easily and there’s a big amount of housing that would be unlocked,” says Hodgkinson.

Emma Ramell, at the Home Builders Federation, says the system is under serious strain but stops short of endorsing abolition.

That is despite acknowledging that “the model has increasingly failed” in recent years, which has led to developments stalling and “much-needed affordable homes sitting empty”.

Ian McDermott, the chief executive of Peabody and chairman of G15, which acts on behalf of London housing associations, acknowledges that the system “isn’t currently working” but rejects the idea of scrapping it entirely.

“The suggestion that section 106 is a tax on development misses both its purpose and its value,” he says, arguing that the policy must be made to “work better” rather than scrapped.

For McDermott, the system is faltering because housing associations are asked to buy homes after key decisions are made, increasing costs down the line.

He believes closer collaboration between developers and housing associations would ensure such homes are fit for purpose.

But Hodgkinson argues a return to the grant model would encourage more private capital into the affordable homes market.

“Incentivise developers to build affordable homes where they are needed and where they are most cost-efficient through flat grants,” he says.

“We also want to drastically expand the eligibility criteria because developers can and will probably want to build these affordable homes, but private equity or housing associations might also wish to get in on the action.”

Labour has shown little appetite for the type of structural overhaul the think tank is proposing, with a stated focus on long-term reform for the section 106 market.

But with housing starts at a fraction of what is needed and Labour’s 1.5 million homes pledge looking further out of reach, the pressure to find more levers to pull will not dissipate any time soon.

data.title: How to make your investments Trump-proof
data.description:
There was a grim inevitability to the everything, everywhere market rally of the last few years turning into the (nearly) everything, everywhere market sell-off.

Most stock markets have shot up in value in the past few years, including, whisper it quietly, our own.

Then along came the Iran war, and many of those gains vanished. That leaves investors with a more tactical mindset in a quandary: even after the ceasefire, how to hedge against the certainty of future uncertainty?

None of us knows what will happen with Iran, including whether the ceasefire will hold. But we do know, with some degree of uncertainty, that inflation is increasing, food crises are looming and energy costs have risen sharply. How to manage this risk?

A good place to start is with the evidence so far. This defies conventional investment logic, proving that the past is no guide to the future. Long-trusted hedges against geopolitical risk and market uncertainty have crumbled on contact.

No major asset class offered any meaningful protection before the ceasefire: not bonds, not gold, not tech stocks or even domestically oriented UK stocks via the FTSE 250 index.

Nearly everything has fallen, and none of the traditional hedges has worked – with one obvious exception, namely, global energy stocks, with the MSCI World energy index up 6.8pc. Even this last sector has stumbled in the last few days as markets begin to think through a possible deal.

That said, even if there is an eventual deal over Iran, most commentators expect the volatility in energy markets to remain high, and prices to remain above levels seen before the conflict. That suggests energy stocks might still be a useful hedge.

The flip side of this strength is that those bumper profits will almost certainly attract political attention.

The strategist Louis-Vincent Gave, of Gavekal, the widely followed research firm, warned last week of blowback against the energy industry.

He wrote to clients that in “Europe, this might mean one-off taxes on exceptional profits. In the US ... it might mean price controls and export restrictions”.

As Gave noted, “incidentally, and perhaps unsurprisingly, China already seems to be there”, with restrictions on exports of fuel and fertilisers.

For me, the interesting failure so far has been hedge funds: all the major hedge fund strategies, as covered by the HFRU composite indices, fell away in March, with the biggest losses for global macro strategies, ie investors trying to second-guess big structural shifts.

This, I think, underscores the uniqueness of the current Trump-led world. Who would try to second-guess what the US president says in his next Truth Social message?

Trend-following strategies, commonly known as managed futures or CTA strategies, are also struggling as market narratives shift so quickly. One month, we were all set for lower interest rates; next month, those rates are suddenly heading higher.

That said, there is some academic evidence suggesting that if we are heading into a world of higher inflation and possibly lower growth, hedge funds might be better able to navigate this uncertainty, though I’d attach a big warning to that statement: “depending on what Trump does next”.

That potentially more stagflationary world order – higher, more persistent inflation, and lower GDP growth as consumers swallow increased prices – brings us to two classic hedges against risk: gold and bonds. The sell-off in gold isn’t a huge surprise to most observers. Gold and silver had become hype-driven and had overshot, and the correction is being driven by huge retail investors selling of ETFs.

That underscores an observation from the recent, seminal UBS Global Investment Returns Yearbook by UK academics. This found that “of the 28 years in which inflation exceeded 3pc, we find that gold returns were negative in 13 of them”. Nevertheless, the academics balanced this by saying that their data show “that gold has been more effective at beating inflation over the long term”.

Put simply, don’t lazily assume that if inflation rises over the next 12 months, gold prices will shoot up again, although gold remains a useful diversifier if inflation rates stay consistently high for a number of years. Also, gold bulls argue that it might benefit from investor concerns about ever-expanding fiscal deficits among the major G7 governments.

Which brings us to the classic diversifier of risk: bonds. Bonds haven’t provided quite the protection that you might have expected, bar inflation-linked securities, which have held their own in price terms.

If the outcome of the present crisis is higher inflation, then most conventional bonds could be in trouble. In fact, I find it surprising how few strategists I talk to think bonds have much value beyond inflation-linked bonds, which could be a contrarian signal if you are a cynic about pointy-head types.

However, returning to that UBS study, I would point out that the academics crunched data for 125 years during periods of high and low inflation and found that bonds provided negative real returns in all scenarios where inflation was high, while equities outperformed, and bonds only provided positive real returns where inflation was “middling or lower”, as the academics put it.

As an aside, the real killer scenario for bonds is one where inflation is high and economic growth rates much lower – a scenario that grows more likely by the day.

I’d finish with two suggestions, one based on the stagflation scenario, the other on increased market turbulence. If you think stagflation is very likely, then a recent analysis by Schroders’ Duncan Lamont is a useful guide.

He’s dug around in similar data sets to those of UBS researchers and found that, in a stagflationary world, equities generally perform worse during stagflation than at other times, but the difference is not statistically meaningful, and they have historically kept pace with inflation and beaten cash.

In terms of sectors, investors might reasonably expect energy/mining, utilities, and healthcare stocks to do well, while avoiding tech and financials.

I’m not completely convinced by the stagflation arguments. We could, for instance, lunge into a recession, or AI could produce a burst of growth. Not every scenario points to stagflation.

But plenty of scenarios in the next few years do point to my main worry: market turbulence driven by Mr Trump.

That will prompt many peaks and troughs, which will be fantastic news for my last suggested hedge: the broking businesses that make money from turbulent markets.

AJ Bell is up 12.8pc in March; CMC Markets up 6.83pc; and Plus500 up just under 2pc. This means they have proved fantastic hedges in the current market turmoil.

These businesses make more money not only as interest rates rise but as investors trade more often. They are my preferred, highly profitable, cash-generating hedges.

data.title: Building in London is becoming impossible, warns housing giant
data.description:
Building homes in London is becoming impossible because of high taxes and red tape, a major housebuilder has warned.

Berkeley Group said on Wednesday that it was halting new land purchases and reducing investment in its building projects, most of which are in the capital or other urban areas.

The company said the conflict in Iran, which is pushing up costs, and a deteriorating economic outlook contributed to its decision to pull back from the market.

However, Rob Perrins, the chief executive of Berkeley, criticised red tape and taxes that he said risked making London effectively uninvestable.

Berkeley said land prices were becoming “overheated” because it was too easy for other businesses to bid for land. Mr Perrins said that Big Yellow Group, the self-storage giant, is his “biggest competitor at the moment”.

“The Government needs to prioritise housebuilding land and not allow Big Yellow to buy all the land, because London will have no [new] homes, fundamentally, if we carry on as we are,” he said.

“You can’t keep increasing taxation on fewer and fewer houses. We have three types of taxes that are too high – development tax, customer-facing tax and corporate tax, which other land uses don’t get.”

In its update to the market, Berkeley criticised “an unprecedented increase in cost and regulation, at a time of increasing interest rates and faltering consumer confidence, amidst prolonged geopolitical and macro-economic volatility and uncertainty”.

Mr Perrins criticised the building safety levy, a tax aimed at funding cladding remediation work and the community infrastructure levy, a charge that councils use to fund infrastructure such as schools and health services.

He said: “The building safety levy should be dropped. We already pay a residential property developer tax of 4pc. Our corporation tax is [effectively] 29pc. We’ve got all the employee taxes.

“There’s a huge amount of what I call customer-facing taxes [such as] stamp duty, and then there’s a huge amount of development tax.

“The Government is not focusing and prioritising affordable housing, [but] focusing on tax-raising measures like the Community Infrastructure Levy.”

Building safety planning rules had added around a year to the time taken to gain planning approval and begin construction on projects, Berkeley said.

Mr Perrins said the quagmire in London meant Labour’s manifesto pledge to build 1.5 million homes by the end of this parliament was looking increasingly far-fetched.

Underpinning that ambition is the Mayor of London’s target to build 88,000 homes a year in the capital.

Mr Perrins said: “If London has a requirement for 88,000 homes, and we’re building 10pc of those, it probably sums up how difficult it is for the Government to hit its target. It just puts it in complete context.”

Steve Reed, the Housing Secretary and Sir Sadiq Khan, the Mayor of London, unveiled an emergency package to boost housebuilding in the capital in October. Reforms include cutting the proportion of affordable homes required for a site from 35pc to 20pc.

Mr Perrins said the temporary measures were to be “applauded”, but warned their success would depend entirely on how “forward-thinking” each borough is.

“My worry is, if boroughs don’t want to follow the messaging, it will not change behaviours on the ground,” he said.

“From a policy point of view, I can’t fault [the measures] … but if local authorities won’t implement them, then it will not translate into new houses quickly.”

He pointed to recent efforts to build 586 homes in Kingston, which were refused by the council after five years of negotiations.

Mr Perrins said the Government needed to consider more measures, such as reducing taxes and regulation on builders, and bringing in demand stimulus through Help to Buy-style schemes for buyers and stamp duty reductions.

Shares in Berkeley plummeted by nearly 18pc on Wednesday morning, marking the steepest fall in a decade. The stock has fallen 24pc over the last month.

The housebuilder cut its profit forecasts following the update. It now expects to generate £1.4bn of pre-tax profit in the four years between 2027 and 2030, roughly equivalent to £350m a year.

Anthony Codling of RBC Capital Markets said the new forecast was around a third lower than previous estimates.

Berkeley said it would redirect its focus to developing its existing land holdings and buying plots through joint-venture agreements, rather than on its own.

Its existing land holdings consist of sites for more than 50,000 homes, with a further pipeline of more than 10,000 in London and the South East.

Aynsley Lammin of Investec said London was the “most difficult area” in the UK for housebuilding and said Berkeley’s decisions were “reflective of the difficult situations” builders face in the capital.

“The affordability constraints are greater. If you’re a first-time buyer, just the hurdle to raise a deposit to get the mortgage is that much more difficult in London,” he said.

Data published by Nationwide in January showed the average first-time buyer must put down a deposit of £44,800 for a home, well above the national average of £23,000.

Mr Lammin said: “Investor appetite has waned as well, particularly from overseas investors, with all of the regulations on investment properties, buy-to-let - that’s been a big factor for Berkeley Group. London was more of an investor market and that’s become very difficult.

“Developers also face more regulatory and cost burdens in London… Some of it is obviously economy-wide, but it’s concentrated and magnified in London. Everything just becomes that much more difficult.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Tudor manor refurb plan branded ‘poor taxidermy’
data.description:
Plans to redevelop a Tudor manor house have been criticised as “poor taxidermy” because the interior is expected to be completely rebuilt.

The derelict Winstanley Hall, near Wigan, which dates back to around 1573, has been empty since previous regeneration plans fell through 40 years ago.

However, new plans to renovate the site and turn the building into 36 apartments and houses have been criticised by neighbours.

Syd Cottle, from the Save Winstanley Greenbelt campaign, said: “Only the facade will be retained, so what you’ll have is a brand new building replacing the internal heritage aspects of the structure.

“I compare it to the building equivalent of poor taxidermy.”

Winstanley Hall was owned for 400 years by the Bankes, a successful coal-mining family during the 18th and early 19th centuries. The house was last occupied in the mid-1980s.

Since then, the structure has deteriorated significantly from a lack of maintenance and slates being stolen from the roof, which led to water damage and the collapse of the building’s interior.

A spokesman from Historic England said: “We are pleased to finally see a proposal for what remains of the house and outbuildings to be retained and converted to new homes by a developer.

“However, we remain concerned that the site has been allowed to deteriorate for so many years to its current condition – despite its high national listed status.”

According to Historic England, Grade II* buildings are particularly important buildings of more than special interest. Around 5.8 per cent of listed buildings are Grade II*.

Last year, Kingswood Homes bought the site and has since submitted plans to Wigan council to redevelop it and create nearly 400 new homes.

Locals have objected to the plans, and a petition regarding the proposal has received more than 1,000 signatures.

Deborah Orr, from the Save Winstanley Greenbelt campaign, said there seemed to be a “build, baby, build ethos”, which “feels like it is not properly thought out”.

She said: “We don’t want them to build loads of houses that are not going to be sustainable, will cause flooding, traffic, overcrowding and put pressure on our services.

“The area already floods a lot. Where is the analysis of adding a concrete jungle on that?”

Hayley Pierce, the Green Party candidate for Standish ward in Wigan, said the plans would see the old hall “gutted”.

She added: “The planned proposal for development on the site on and around Winstanley Hall is not a restoration that would bring the hall into use for the community, it is a plan to gut the old hall and fill it with new build flats.”

‘Last chance of being saved’

Paul Jones, the managing director of Kingswood Homes, said: “We are trying to save the hall and bring forward social housing as we are in a time where there’s a massive housing shortage.

“We want to restore the hall and save it, and it is its last chance of being saved.

“People can claim that it’s not a restoration, but Historic England has said it is a restoration and it is supportive of it.

“All those concerns will be assessed by the council as part of the application. If there is an impact, then it has to be mitigated.”

Aidan Thatcher, director of place at Wigan council, said: “At this stage, no decisions have been made regarding the developer’s plans at Winstanley Hall and the surrounding area. 

“We are committed to a thorough and transparent planning process and will review all relevant information, including views from the public, before making a final decision.”

data.title: Sadiq Khan could wage war on ‘Chelsea tractor’ owners with new charges
data.description:
Sir Sadiq Khan is considering waging a war on “Chelsea tractor” owners with new charges, a new document suggests.

Transport for London’s (TfL) Vision Zero action plan claimed there was a “pressing need for targeted safety interventions” against larger vehicles.

It stated the size of large SUVs – nicknamed “Chelsea tractors” – made roads “more hazardous for everybody outside the vehicle” because they “significantly increase the severity of collisions”.

The plan is part of Sir Sadiq’s efforts to eliminate deaths and serious injuries on London’s roads by 2041.

There is no specific definition of an SUV, but it is a term given to vehicles that are generally taller, wider, heavier and less fuel-efficient than traditional cars. Critics have pointed out that electric vehicles (EVs) are just as heavy, but would not be affected by the plans.

Thomas Turrell, a member of the London Assembly, said: “This family car tax is a double standard. The mayor will not go after his precious heavy EV cars with this regressive, backwards tax, instead choosing to hammer families.

“His anti-car zealotry will not stop here. If he truly believes vehicles should be taxed for being heavier, he should be consistent rather than money-grabbing.”

Mike Hawes, the chief executive of the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders, said all cars sold in the UK “regardless of size” must meet “exacting safety and pedestrian protection standards”.

He added: “Singling out specific cars based on size restricts consumer choice and would unfairly penalise the many drivers who require a larger vehicle for essential mobility.”

No proposals about possible charges for SUVs have been put forward yet.

SUVs account for about a third of all new cars sold in the UK. They have grown in popularity in recent years, with many drivers favouring their higher seating position.

The Vision Zero document claimed “oversized” vehicles such as SUVs made it “harder for drivers to see people walking and cycling nearby”, adding that children were “more easily hidden from a driver’s view”.

It stated that in Britain, SUVs were 14 per cent more likely to kill pedestrians and cyclists than other passenger cars if there was a collision, and 77 per cent more likely to kill children.

Punishing London’s motorists

Sir Sadiq expanded the ultra-low-emission zone to cover the whole of London in August 2023, which requires drivers of vehicles that do not meet emissions standards to pay a daily fee of £12.50.

London also has an £18 congestion charge for driving in a zone in the centre of the capital at certain times.

Sir Sadiq has faced sustained criticism from ordinary Londoners that he ignores their concerns about schemes that punish motorists.

One such scheme was formally approved this week, which involves the construction of a cycle path at the Holland Park roundabout in west London, one of the capital’s busiest road interchanges.

Residents have repeatedly objected to the £6m scheme and have claimed that TfL has cited false statistics generated to justify the works.

Ruth Saunders, a member of the Save Our Streets group based near the roundabout, said: “This scheme is designed to benefit a small number of commuter cyclists travelling through the borough for a few hours each day, to the detriment of local residents and other road users.”

Regarding the potential plans to tax SUVs, a spokesman for Sir Sadiq claimed: “There is growing evidence about the safety risks of large SUVs, which are becoming more common on London’s roads.

“The mayor has asked TfL to undertake detailed analysis of the safety risks posed by large SUVs and their wider impact on London’s roads.

“This could then inform any future policy proposals in London or recommendations to Government or the sector.

“As the numbers of large SUVs increase on London’s roads, it is right that TfL consider their impact on safety and how they affect other road users.”

data.title: Net zero zealots are putting Gen Z off building planes
data.description:
Britain’s aerospace companies are gearing up for their biggest boom in a generation.

Record airliner orders will require plane makers and their suppliers to lift production by a fifth in the next few years, creating well-paid jobs for thousands of workers.

Yet a UK aerospace industry that produced the jet engine, the Harrier jump jet and Concorde is being hampered by a growing recruitment crisis.

Years of demonisation by green campaigners such as Greta Thunberg for its inability to wean itself off fossil fuels are beginning to take their toll as young people turn away from careers in industry.

A lack of inspirational new aircraft and the allure of rival jobs in technology and AI are compounding the situation, leaving even the biggest firms struggling to hire.

Airbus, which employs 15,000 people in the UK, is resorting to converting one of its Beluga wing transporters into a recruitment centre in an effort to bolster its appeal to Gen Z.

The firm will fit the plane with classrooms and a mock-up of its assembly line as it seeks to recruit hundreds of apprentices a year to the Broughton wing plant in North Wales.

Bosses are hoping that Nasa’s Artemis programme to return humans to the Moon by 2028 will also help restore some of the wider industry’s lost glamour.

Richard Gardner, the honorary president of Farnborough Air Sciences Trust, said Gen Z had been turned off aviation by eco-zealots who wrongly condemn the sector for its environmental impact.

He suggested the problem starts in school, where children are increasingly taught about the impacts of climate change.

He said: “We talk about the word brainwashing, which is quite emotive for some people, but that is what it is.

“The teaching unions are very influential in terms of the way that profession is going, and they are pretty green in their tendencies.”

Gardner added: “I think they mostly buy into these green policies, science and economics teachers aside, and that aerospace and defence are therefore not high in their career recommendations.”

He said the recent rise of the Green Party as an electoral force is also a “big worry” for aviation.

Green policies include a commitment to banning domestic flights for journeys that could be made by train, halting airport expansion and imposing a tax on frequent travellers.

Kevin Craven, the chief executive of ADS, the trade group for aerospace, defence and space firms, said that while the industry might not be decarbonising fast enough for some young people, its focus on reducing emissions may be failing to motivate others.

He said: “There is a feeling that aerospace has faded into the background a bit because there are no longer any mountains being climbed.

“Concorde, the Comet, the jet engine and the Harrier were all British firsts that really excited and inspired people.”

Craven added: “All new aircraft are really a cylinder with wings and look approximately the same. There are incremental advances which are huge for sustainability, but it’s hard to see that progression.

“There is also huge competition from other high-tech arenas – particularly AI – and, more generally, the City, where vast swathes of money are available to the best and brightest.”

Mr Craven said it was galling to see tech companies recruiting the most able engineers, even though their electricity consumption is set to double by 2030, creating clear parallels with the net zero challenges facing aviation.

He said: “When you look at cloud computing, on the other side of it is a dirty big data centre requiring huge amounts of power and huge amounts of cooling.”

‘Incredibly competitive world’

Sue Partridge, the UK country manager for Airbus, said the world’s biggest plane maker had turned to initiatives such as the Beluga recruitment project as it prepares to deliver 1,000 jet planes a year as early as 2027, up from 793 in 2025.

Airbus’s Filton, Gloucestershire, wing design and development centre near Bristol faces competition for A-level students and graduate recruits from a broad spectrum of rival employers, she said.

The firm, which has trained 4,000 British recruits in the past decade, also faces a fight to hire 16-year-old apprentices for its assembly line in Broughton, Flintshire, with Merseyside’s carmakers and the cluster of green energy firms on nearby Deeside, Wales.

She said: “It’s an incredibly competitive world out there, and we need to do everything we can to attract talent and secure those skills we are going to need for the future.

“But it is difficult, particularly in newer digital skill areas, where we are competing with Google and companies like that, which was never the case in the past.”

Partridge added: “With the younger cohort, the challenge is attracting them to the industry in the first place and inspiring them so that they want to actually build aeroplanes. They get influenced in their career choice at an early age.

“A few years ago, all the stories were about flight shaming, and aerospace was the bad guy. That’s why we want to get into schools so they really connect with Airbus and aerospace manufacturing.”

The Beluga, named after the white whale whose bloated fuselage it resembles, garners rare attention for a modern plane and would be a formidable tool in encouraging take-up of Stem (science, technology, engineering and mathematics) subjects, Ms Partridge said.

“There are still inspirational stories we can tell, and I think the Beluga is one,” she said. “We know people love the plane, and we can use that as a symbol.

“It’s an iconic aircraft, and if you see one in the sky, for me, it’s just as impactful as Concorde used to be. We already have a Concorde near Filton, South Gloucestershire, and we can use this in the same way.”

Mr Craven said the development of so-called flying taxis, in which Britain is playing a leading role, is one area that should resonate with younger people.

That is largely “because they will be silent, and there won’t be obvious pollutants pouring out of the back”.

However, it is not clear how big the market for the electric vertical take-off craft will be, with some industry observers concerned that they will be limited to whisking the very rich to and from first-class flights, a role hardly likely to endear them to Gen Z.

Industry experts hope that a new era of manned space travel could achieve greater cut-through.

Elon Musk’s SpaceX has made space a “much closer frontier”, while the Artemis missions have the potential to capture hearts and minds just as the Apollo programme did in the 1960s and 1970s, said Mr Craven.

He said: “Aerospace and space are joined at the hip, and whenever we ask an astronaut to an event, there is a frisson of excitement. People want heroes, and that combination of high-tech and courage is the ultimate inspiration.

“I can remember watching the Moon landing on a black and white TV with a 10-inch screen in the middle of Africa. It’s probably why I ended up as an engineer.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Starbucks blames minimum wage and taxes for mounting losses
data.description:
Starbucks has blamed higher jobs taxes and minimum wage under Labour for mounting losses at its UK business.

The US coffee chain said “changes to minimum wage and employer National Insurance contributions” had put pressure on its profit margins as operating losses widened from £27.5m to £29.8m.

Staff costs rose from £118m to £129m in the year to October 2025, even as the UK business cut more than 200 jobs to take its headcount to just over 5,320.

Starbucks made an operating loss in the UK despite turnover rising by £30m to £556.3m, according to accounts filed at Companies House.

It is the latest business to warn of the impact of higher employment costs under Labour. Bosses have warned that changes to minimum wage and National Insurance will lead to fewer jobs and prompt hospitality businesses to cut opening hours.

Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, mounted a £25bn raid on employer’s National Insurance in her 2024 Budget and has overseen successive inflation-busting increases in the minimum wage. The latest saw the headline rate rise by 4.1pc at the start of this month, well ahead of the 3pc rate of inflation in February.

As well as increased employment costs, Starbucks said a “challenging consumer environment” marked by reduced spending and higher costs also contributed to the tough year.

Prices for coffee, energy and milk all rose. A company source said that Starbucks had faced cost increases of up to 10pc, as poor weather in Brazil and higher freight costs drove up wholesale coffee bean prices.

While the UK business made an operating loss, it recorded a post-tax profit of £30m after banking a £71m tax credit. The company said it had unused allowances from previous years and was now claiming them to help rebuild cash and fund investment in the business.

The coffee giant launched a £750m restructuring plan last year that led to 900 job cuts globally and plans to close scores of cafes. Around 77 locations were closed in the UK.

The restructuring came after a difficult period for Starbucks, which has been criticised for its high prices.

Starbucks said in its accounts that it remained confident of its strategy, while warning that coffee, milk, and energy remained expensive. The annual report did not mention the war in Iran, which is expected to fuel further inflation.

Hospitality firms are particularly exposed to rising energy costs, and separately face sharp rises in business rates in the UK.

Starbucks was approached for comment.
data.author: The telegraph
